title: Contains Duplicate
summary: Given an integer array, determine whether any value appears at least twice.
approachSummary: A value repeats if and only if inserting it into a set that already contains it fails to grow the set - so a single pass with a set catches any duplicate in O(1) amortized per check.
walkthrough: Create an empty set. Walk through the array once; for each value, check whether it's already in the set. If it is, a duplicate exists and you can return true immediately. Otherwise add it to the set and continue. If you reach the end without a hit, return false.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass, O(1) average set operations
complexity.space: O(n) - the set can hold up to n elements

pitfalls: Sorting first also works but costs O(n log n) - the set approach is asymptotically better
Remember to check membership before inserting, not after

title: Valid Anagram
summary: Given two strings, determine whether the second is an anagram of the first.
approachSummary: Two strings are anagrams exactly when they have the same multiset of characters, which a single character-frequency count can verify in linear time.
walkthrough: If the lengths differ, return false immediately. Otherwise build a frequency count of characters in the first string, then walk the second string decrementing counts. If any count goes negative, they're not anagrams. At the end all counts should be zero.

complexity.time: O(n) - two linear passes over the strings
complexity.space: O(1) - at most 26 lowercase letters, or O(k) for a general alphabet of size k

pitfalls: Forgetting the early length check wastes a pass
Unicode/case-sensitivity assumptions should be stated explicitly

title: Two Sum
summary: Given an array of integers and a target, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to the target.
approachSummary: Instead of checking every pair, track values you've already seen so that for each new number you can check in O(1) whether its complement has already appeared.
walkthrough: Iterate through the array once, keeping a map from value to index. For each number, compute target minus that number; if it's already in the map, you've found your pair - return the stored index and the current index. Otherwise store the current value's index and continue.

complexity.time: O(n) - single pass with O(1) hash map operations
complexity.space: O(n) - the map can hold up to n entries

pitfalls: Check for the complement before inserting the current value, or you can match a number with itself incorrectly
Assuming the array is sorted (it isn't guaranteed to be)

title: Valid Palindrome
summary: Given a string, determine whether it reads the same forward and backward after ignoring non-alphanumeric characters and case.
approachSummary: Two pointers closing in from both ends let you compare characters directly without building a cleaned copy of the string first.
walkthrough: Set a left pointer at the start and a right pointer at the end. Advance left past any non-alphanumeric characters, and retreat right past any non-alphanumeric characters. Compare the two characters case-insensitively; if they differ, it's not a palindrome. Move both pointers inward and repeat until they cross.

complexity.time: O(n) - each character is visited a constant number of times
complexity.space: O(1) - only two index variables

pitfalls: Forgetting to skip non-alphanumeric characters on both sides independently
Case sensitivity - remember to normalize case before comparing

title: 3Sum
summary: Given an integer array, find all unique triplets that sum to zero.
approachSummary: Sorting first turns the search for triplets summing to zero into, for each fixed first element, a linear two-pointer search over the remainder - avoiding an O(n^3) brute force and making duplicate skipping straightforward.
walkthrough: Sort the array. For each index i, skip it if it's the same as the previous element (to avoid duplicate triplets). Then run a two-pointer search over the remaining subarray: move left and right inward, and when the three values sum to zero record the triplet and skip past any duplicate values on both sides before continuing.

complexity.time: O(n^2) - O(n log n) sort plus a two-pointer scan for each of n elements
complexity.space: O(n) or O(log n) depending on the sort's space usage, excluding the output

pitfalls: Duplicate triplets are easy to introduce if you don't skip repeated values at all three positions
Off-by-one errors moving the two pointers past duplicates

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
summary: Given an array of daily stock prices, find the maximum profit from buying on one day and selling on a later day.
approachSummary: Track the lowest price seen so far as you scan forward; the best profit at any point is today's price minus that running minimum.
walkthrough: Initialize the minimum price to the first element and the best profit to zero. Walk through the remaining prices: if the current price is lower than the running minimum, update the minimum. Otherwise compute the profit from selling today and update the best profit if it's higher.

complexity.time: O(n) - single pass
complexity.space: O(1) - two running variables

pitfalls: You cannot sell before you buy - the running minimum must only reflect prices seen so far, not the whole array
An empty price list needs a defined result (typically 0)

title: Valid Parentheses
summary: Given a string of brackets, determine whether every bracket is closed by the correct matching type in the correct order.
approachSummary: A stack naturally mirrors the nesting structure of brackets: the most recently opened bracket must be the next one closed.
walkthrough: Walk through the string. Push opening brackets onto a stack. On a closing bracket, check that the stack is non-empty and its top matches the expected opening bracket for this closer; if so pop it, otherwise the string is invalid. At the end, the string is valid only if the stack is empty.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass through the string
complexity.space: O(n) - the stack can hold up to n opening brackets

pitfalls: Forgetting to check the stack isn't empty before popping on a closing bracket
Forgetting the final check that the stack is empty at the end (unmatched opening brackets)

title: Binary Search
summary: Given a sorted array and a target value, return the target's index, or -1 if it isn't present.
approachSummary: Because the array is sorted, comparing the target to the middle element lets you discard half the remaining search space on every step.
walkthrough: Maintain low and high bounds spanning the whole array. While low <= high, compute the midpoint, compare the target to the value there. If equal, return the index. If the target is smaller, move high to just below the midpoint; if larger, move low to just above it. If the bounds cross without a match, the target isn't present.

complexity.time: O(log n) - the search space halves each iteration
complexity.space: O(1) - iterative, no extra structures

pitfalls: Computing the midpoint as (low + high) // 2 can overflow in some languages (not a concern in Python, but worth knowing)
Off-by-one errors updating low/high after a comparison

title: Group Anagrams
summary: Given an array of strings, group the ones that are anagrams of each other.
approachSummary: Anagrams share the same sorted character sequence, so that sorted string makes a natural hash map key to bucket words together.
walkthrough: Create a map from a signature to a list of words. For each word, compute a signature - either its sorted characters or a 26-length character count tuple - and append the word to the list for that signature. At the end, the map's values are the grouped anagrams.

complexity.time: O(n * k log k) - n words, each sorted in O(k log k) for word length k (or O(n * k) using a count-based signature)
complexity.space: O(n * k) - storing all words and their signatures

pitfalls: A count-tuple signature avoids the log k sort factor for large words
Remember the output is a list of lists, not a map

title: Top K Frequent Elements
summary: Given an integer array, return the k most frequently occurring elements.
approachSummary: Counting frequencies then bucketing values by frequency lets you read off the top k in linear time without a full sort.
walkthrough: Count each value's frequency with a hash map. Create buckets indexed 0..n where bucket[f] holds all values with frequency f. Walk the buckets from highest frequency down, collecting values until you have k of them.

complexity.time: O(n) - counting and bucketing are both linear in the input size
complexity.space: O(n) - the frequency map and buckets

pitfalls: A heap-based approach works too but costs O(n log k) instead of O(n)
Frequency can be at most n, so buckets only need n+1 slots

title: Product of Array Except Self
summary: Given an array, return a new array where each element is the product of all the other elements, without using division.
approachSummary: The product of everything except index i is the product of all prefix values before i times the product of all suffix values after i, computed without division.
walkthrough: Make one pass left to right building a running prefix product into the result array (result[i] = product of everything before i). Then make a second pass right to left, multiplying each result[i] by a running suffix product of everything after i.

complexity.time: O(n) - two linear passes
complexity.space: O(1) extra space excluding the output array

pitfalls: Division-based solutions break when the array contains a zero
The output array itself can double as the prefix buffer to save space
solutionCode:
def product_except_self(nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    n = len(nums)
    result = [1] * n
    prefix = 1
    for i in range(n):
        result[i] = prefix
        prefix *= nums[i]
    suffix = 1
    for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
        result[i] *= suffix
        suffix *= nums[i]
    return result

title: Valid Sudoku
summary: Given a partially filled 9x9 Sudoku board, determine whether it's valid so far under standard Sudoku rules.
approachSummary: A board is valid exactly when no row, column, or 3x3 box has a repeated digit, which a set of seen (value, row), (value, col), and (value, box) markers can check in one pass.
walkthrough: Walk every cell once. For each filled cell, compute its box index from row // 3 and col // 3. Check three sets - one for rows, one for columns, one for boxes - for the digit already being present; if so, the board is invalid. Otherwise add the digit to all three sets and continue.

complexity.time: O(1) - the board is always 9x9, so the work is bounded by a constant
complexity.space: O(1) - a constant number of sets bounded by the fixed board size

pitfalls: Remember to key box membership by (row // 3, col // 3), not just row or col alone
Skip empty cells rather than treating them as duplicates
solutionCode:
from collections import defaultdict

def is_valid_sudoku(board: list[list[str]]) -> bool:
    rows = defaultdict(set)
    cols = defaultdict(set)
    boxes = defaultdict(set)
    for i in range(9):
        for j in range(9):
            val = board[i][j]
            if val == '.':
                continue
            box = (i // 3, j // 3)
            if val in rows[i] or val in cols[j] or val in boxes[box]:
                return False
            rows[i].add(val)
            cols[j].add(val)
            boxes[box].add(val)
    return True

title: Encode and Decode Strings
summary: Design a way to encode a list of strings into one string and decode it back into the original list.
approachSummary: Prefixing each string with its length and a delimiter lets decode unambiguously split the concatenated blob back into the original strings, even if the strings themselves contain arbitrary characters.
walkthrough: To encode, for each string write its length, a separator character like '#', then the string itself, and concatenate all of these. To decode, scan for the next '#', parse the number before it as the length, then read exactly that many characters as the next string, and repeat until the input is consumed.

complexity.time: O(n) - linear in the total length of all strings for both encode and decode
complexity.space: O(n) - the encoded blob and the decoded list

pitfalls: A plain delimiter like a comma breaks if strings can contain that character - length-prefixing avoids that
Off-by-one errors reading the length digits before the '#' are a common bug

title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
summary: Given an unsorted integer array, find the length of the longest run of consecutive integers, in O(n) time.
approachSummary: Putting all values in a set lets you find, in O(1), whether a value is the start of a run (its predecessor is absent) and then count forward through the run without sorting.
walkthrough: Add every number to a set. For each number that has no predecessor (num - 1 not in the set), it starts a run - count forward while num + 1, num + 2, ... are in the set, tracking the run length. Skip numbers that do have a predecessor, since they'll be covered by their run's start.

complexity.time: O(n) - each number is visited a constant number of times across all runs
complexity.space: O(n) - the hash set of all values

pitfalls: Sorting first works but costs O(n log n) - the set approach is asymptotically better
Without the predecessor check, you'd recount the same run from every element, going quadratic

title: Two Sum II
summary: Given a 1-indexed array already sorted in ascending order, find two numbers that add up to a target.
approachSummary: Because the array is sorted, a pointer at each end can close inward: increase the left pointer when the sum is too small, decrease the right pointer when it's too large.
walkthrough: Set left at the start and right at the end. While left < right, compute the sum. If it equals the target, return the 1-indexed pair. If it's less than the target, move left forward to increase the sum; if greater, move right backward to decrease it.

complexity.time: O(n) - each pointer moves at most n times total
complexity.space: O(1) - two index variables

pitfalls: The problem asks for 1-indexed positions, not 0-indexed
Assuming duplicates need special handling - they don't here since only one solution is guaranteed

title: Container with Most Water
summary: Given an array of heights representing vertical lines, find two lines that together with the x-axis hold the most water.
approachSummary: The container's area is limited by the shorter of its two walls, so moving the pointer at the shorter wall inward is the only move that can possibly increase the area.
walkthrough: Start pointers at both ends. At each step compute the area as the distance between pointers times the shorter height, updating the best seen. Move whichever pointer points at the shorter wall inward, since keeping it fixed can never produce a taller container.

complexity.time: O(n) - each pointer moves inward at most n times total
complexity.space: O(1) - two index variables and a running best

pitfalls: Moving the taller pointer can only shrink the width without helping height, so it never improves the answer
The area uses the shorter height, not the sum or average of both

title: Trapping Rain Water
summary: Given an array representing an elevation map, compute how much rainwater it can trap after raining.
approachSummary: Water trapped above any bar is bounded by the shorter of the tallest wall to its left and the tallest wall to its right, which two inward-moving pointers can track without precomputing both arrays.
walkthrough: Track a running max height seen from the left and from the right. Move the pointer on the side with the smaller running max inward, since that side's max determines the water level at the current position; add the difference between that max and the current bar's height (if positive) to the total.

complexity.time: O(n) - each pointer moves inward at most n times total
complexity.space: O(1) - two running maxima and two pointers

pitfalls: Precomputing left-max and right-max arrays also works but uses O(n) extra space; the two-pointer version is O(1)
Water only accumulates where the running max exceeds the current bar's height
solutionCode:
def trap(height: list[int]) -> int:
    left, right = 0, len(height) - 1
    left_max, right_max = 0, 0
    result = 0
    while left < right:
        if height[left] < height[right]:
            left_max = max(left_max, height[left])
            result += left_max - height[left]
            left += 1
        else:
            right_max = max(right_max, height[right])
            result += right_max - height[right]
            right -= 1
    return result

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
summary: Given a string, find the length of the longest substring that contains no repeated characters.
approachSummary: A window that expands to include new characters and contracts from the left whenever a duplicate appears always tracks the longest repeat-free substring ending at the current position.
walkthrough: Keep a set of characters currently in the window and a left pointer. For each right pointer position, while the character is already in the window, remove the character at the left pointer and advance left. Add the current character to the window and update the best length seen.

complexity.time: O(n) - each character enters and leaves the window at most once
complexity.space: O(min(n, alphabet size)) - the window's character set

pitfalls: A hash map of last-seen index can jump left directly instead of removing one at a time, which is faster but not required for correctness
Resetting the whole window on a duplicate instead of shrinking from the left is a common but incorrect shortcut
solutionCode:
def length_of_longest_substring(s: str) -> int:
    seen: set[str] = set()
    left = 0
    result = 0
    for right, ch in enumerate(s):
        while ch in seen:
            seen.remove(s[left])
            left += 1
        seen.add(ch)
        result = max(result, right - left + 1)
    return result

title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
summary: Given a string and a limit on character replacements, find the longest substring achievable where all characters are the same after at most that many replacements.
approachSummary: A window is achievable with at most k replacements exactly when its length minus the count of its most frequent character is at most k, so the window only ever needs to grow or slide, never shrink permanently.
walkthrough: Maintain a character count for the current window and a running max frequency within it. Expand the window each step; if window length minus the max frequency exceeds k, shrink from the left by one. Track the best window length seen; it never decreases even though max frequency can be stale.

complexity.time: O(n) - each character enters and leaves the window at most once
complexity.space: O(1) - a fixed 26-letter count array

pitfalls: The max-frequency tracker doesn't need to be recomputed exactly on shrink - an overestimate never shrinks the window incorrectly
Forgetting that the window can slide instead of only shrink misses that the best length is monotonic
solutionCode:
from collections import defaultdict

def character_replacement(s: str, k: int) -> int:
    counts = defaultdict(int)
    left = 0
    max_freq = 0
    result = 0
    for right, ch in enumerate(s):
        counts[ch] += 1
        max_freq = max(max_freq, counts[ch])
        window_len = right - left + 1
        if window_len - max_freq > k:
            counts[s[left]] -= 1
            left += 1
        else:
            result = max(result, window_len)
    return result

title: Permutation in String
summary: Given two strings, determine whether the second contains a contiguous substring that is a permutation of the first.
approachSummary: A permutation of s1 appears in s2 exactly when some fixed-size window of s2 has the same character-frequency counts as s1, checked with a fixed-size sliding window.
walkthrough: Build a frequency count of s1 and a frequency count of the first window of s2 of the same length. Slide the window one character at a time: add the entering character's count, remove the leaving character's count, and compare the two counts. If they ever match, a permutation exists.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over s2 with O(1) fixed-alphabet comparisons
complexity.space: O(1) - fixed 26-letter count arrays

pitfalls: Comparing full dictionaries on every slide is correct but slower - tracking a running 'matches' counter is a common optimization
The window size must equal len(s1) exactly, not be allowed to vary

title: Minimum Window Substring
summary: Given two strings s and t, find the smallest substring of s that contains every character of t, including duplicates.
approachSummary: Expanding the window until it covers every required character, then greedily contracting from the left while it still covers them, finds the smallest valid window that ever existed.
walkthrough: Build a target character count from t. Expand right, decrementing a remaining-need counter whenever a needed character is consumed. Once all needs are satisfied (need count reaches zero), shrink from the left as far as possible while still satisfying every need, recording the smallest window found, then continue expanding.

complexity.time: O(n + m) - each pointer traverses s at most once, plus building the count from t
complexity.space: O(k) - the count map, bounded by the alphabet of t

pitfalls: Only decrement the 'satisfied' counter when a character's count crosses from positive to zero, not on every occurrence
Forgetting to also try shrinking after the window already satisfies the condition misses smaller valid windows

title: Sliding Window Maximum
summary: Given an array and a window size k, return the maximum value in each sliding window of size k as it moves across the array.
approachSummary: A deque of indices kept in decreasing order of value always has the current window's maximum at its front, since any smaller value behind a larger one can never become the max while the larger one is still in the window.
walkthrough: Maintain a deque of indices whose values are in decreasing order. For each new index, pop from the back while its value is smaller than the incoming value, then push the new index. Pop from the front if it has fallen out of the window's left bound. Once the window is full size k, its front is the max for that window.

complexity.time: O(n) - each index is pushed and popped from the deque at most once
complexity.space: O(k) - the deque holds at most k indices

pitfalls: The deque stores indices, not values, so window-bound checks are possible
Forgetting to pop smaller values from the back before pushing breaks the decreasing invariant

title: Min Stack
summary: Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element, all in O(1) time.
approachSummary: Pairing every pushed value with the current minimum at push time turns getMin into an O(1) lookup at the top of a parallel min-stack.
walkthrough: Maintain a normal value stack and a min-stack. On push, push the value onto the value stack, and push the smaller of the new value and the current min-stack top onto the min-stack. On pop, pop both stacks together. getMin just reads the min-stack's top.

complexity.time: O(1) - every operation touches only the tops of the two stacks
complexity.space: O(n) - the min-stack duplicates one entry per pushed value

pitfalls: Popping only the value stack and forgetting to pop the min-stack desyncs them
Comparing against a single running variable instead of a stack loses the previous minimum after a pop

title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
summary: Given a list of tokens representing an arithmetic expression in Reverse Polish Notation, evaluate it and return the result.
approachSummary: In postfix notation every operator immediately follows its two operands, so pushing operands and popping two whenever an operator appears evaluates the expression in one linear pass.
walkthrough: Walk the tokens. If a token is a number, push it. If it's an operator, pop the top two values (the second-popped is the left operand), apply the operator, and push the result. After processing all tokens, the single remaining stack value is the answer.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass through the tokens with O(1) stack operations
complexity.space: O(n) - the stack can hold up to n operands

pitfalls: Integer division in this problem truncates toward zero, which differs from Python's floor division for negative numbers
The operand popped first is the right-hand operand, not the left

title: Generate Parentheses
summary: Given n pairs of parentheses, generate all combinations of well-formed parenthesis strings.
approachSummary: A parenthesis string built by backtracking stays valid throughout construction if you only add ')' when there are unmatched '(' still open and only add '(' while there's budget remaining.
walkthrough: Recurse with counts of open and close parentheses used so far. Add '(' if open count is below n. Add ')' if close count is below open count (ensuring it closes something already opened). When the string reaches length 2n, record it as a complete valid combination.

complexity.time: O(4^n / sqrt(n)) - bounded by the nth Catalan number of valid combinations
complexity.space: O(n) - recursion depth proportional to the string length

pitfalls: Adding ')' without checking it's less than the open count produces invalid strings like ')('
Forgetting to backtrack (remove the last character) after recursing corrupts subsequent branches

title: Daily Temperatures
summary: Given a list of daily temperatures, return for each day how many days you'd have to wait for a warmer temperature.
approachSummary: A stack of indices with decreasing temperatures lets each new warmer day resolve the wait for every colder day still waiting on the stack, in one pass.
walkthrough: Walk the temperatures keeping a stack of indices whose temperatures haven't yet found a warmer future day. For each new day, while the stack's top index has a lower temperature than today, pop it and record today's index minus that index as its answer. Push the current index.

complexity.time: O(n) - each index is pushed and popped from the stack at most once
complexity.space: O(n) - the stack can hold up to n indices in the worst case

pitfalls: A brute-force nested loop is O(n^2) - the monotonic stack avoids rechecking resolved days
Storing temperatures instead of indices on the stack loses the ability to compute the distance

title: Car Fleet
summary: Given cars' starting positions and speeds heading to the same target, determine how many fleets arrive together.
approachSummary: Sorting cars by starting position from closest to the target and computing each car's time to reach the target reveals fleets as consecutive cars whose time doesn't exceed the car ahead's time.
walkthrough: Sort cars by position descending (closest to target first). For each car compute time = (target - position) / speed. Process in that order with a stack of fleet arrival times: if the current car's time is less than or equal to the stack's top, it merges into that fleet (doesn't push); otherwise it forms a new fleet and its time is pushed.

complexity.time: O(n log n) - dominated by sorting the cars by position
complexity.space: O(n) - the stack of fleet times

pitfalls: A car can never catch up to a slower car ahead of it, only match its pace and merge - it can't pass
Comparing speeds directly instead of arrival times gives the wrong fleets since faster cars starting further back can still merge

title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
summary: Given an array of bar heights, find the area of the largest rectangle that can be formed within the histogram.
approachSummary: For each bar, the largest rectangle using that bar's height extends until the first shorter bar on each side, which a monotonic increasing stack of indices finds in one pass.
walkthrough: Walk the bars keeping a stack of indices with increasing heights. When the current bar is shorter than the stack's top, pop it and compute the rectangle using the popped height, with width spanning from the new stack top (exclusive) to the current index (exclusive). Push the current index. After the pass, process any bars remaining on the stack against the array end.

complexity.time: O(n) - each index is pushed and popped from the stack at most once
complexity.space: O(n) - the stack can hold up to n indices

pitfalls: The width calculation after popping depends on the new top of the stack, not the popped index's neighbors directly
Forgetting to flush the stack at the end misses rectangles extending to the last bar

title: Search a 2D Matrix
summary: Given an m x n matrix where each row is sorted and the first element of each row is greater than the last of the previous row, determine whether a target value exists.
approachSummary: Treating the row-sorted, column-sorted matrix as one flattened sorted array lets a single binary search locate the target by mapping a flat index back to row and column.
walkthrough: Binary search over indices 0 to rows*cols - 1. For each mid index, convert it to row = mid // cols and col = mid % cols, compare the matrix value there to the target, and narrow the search bounds accordingly, just like a normal sorted-array binary search.

complexity.time: O(log(m * n)) - binary search over the flattened matrix size
complexity.space: O(1) - no extra structures, only index math

pitfalls: The matrix property required is that each row's last element is less than the next row's first element - not just each row/column individually sorted
Off-by-one errors converting between flat index and (row, col)

title: Koko Eating Bananas
summary: Given piles of bananas and a time limit in hours, find the minimum constant eating speed that lets Koko finish all piles in time.
approachSummary: As Koko's eating speed increases, the total hours needed only decreases, so the feasibility of a speed is monotonic and binary search can find the minimum speed that meets the deadline.
walkthrough: Binary search the speed between 1 and the largest pile size. For a candidate speed, compute the total hours needed as the sum of ceil(pile / speed) over all piles. If that's within h hours, the speed is feasible - try smaller; otherwise try larger. The answer is the smallest feasible speed.

complexity.time: O(n log m) - n piles checked at each of log(max pile) binary search steps
complexity.space: O(1) - no extra structures beyond the search bounds

pitfalls: Using integer division instead of ceiling division undercounts the hours needed for a pile
Binary searching on the pile index instead of on the eating speed misses the actual monotonic quantity

title: Search Rotated Sorted Array
summary: Given a sorted array that has been rotated at an unknown pivot, find the index of a target value.
approachSummary: At any midpoint of a rotated sorted array, at least one half is guaranteed to still be normally sorted, so checking which half is sorted tells you where the target could be.
walkthrough: At each step compare nums[mid] to nums[low] to determine which half is sorted. If the left half is sorted and the target falls within its range, search left; otherwise search right. Symmetrically if the right half is sorted. Narrow low/high accordingly until found or the range is empty.

complexity.time: O(log n) - the search space halves each iteration despite the rotation
complexity.space: O(1) - iterative with only index variables

pitfalls: Forgetting the rotation point can lie exactly at low or high needs careful boundary comparisons (<=, not just <)
Assuming the whole array is sorted and doing a plain binary search misses the rotation

title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
summary: Given a sorted array rotated at an unknown pivot with no duplicates, find the minimum element.
approachSummary: The minimum is the only element smaller than the array's last element in its sorted run, so comparing the midpoint to the right endpoint tells you which half still contains the rotation point.
walkthrough: Binary search with low and high bounds. If nums[mid] is greater than nums[high], the minimum is to the right of mid, so move low to mid + 1. Otherwise the minimum is at mid or to its left, so move high to mid. When low equals high, that index holds the minimum.

complexity.time: O(log n) - the search space halves each iteration
complexity.space: O(1) - iterative with only index variables

pitfalls: Comparing mid to low instead of to high can miss edge cases where the array isn't rotated at all
Using nums[mid] < nums[high] without the equality edge case needs care when high = mid

title: Time Based Key-Value Store
summary: Design a time-based key-value store that can set a value for a key at a given timestamp and retrieve the value for a key at or before a given timestamp.
approachSummary: Storing each key's (timestamp, value) pairs in a list sorted by insertion order (which is naturally increasing timestamp order) lets get binary search for the latest timestamp not exceeding the query.
walkthrough: Maintain a map from key to a list of (timestamp, value) pairs, appended in set() call order which keeps timestamps increasing. For get(), binary search that key's list for the rightmost timestamp that is <= the query timestamp, and return its value, or empty string if none exists.

complexity.time: O(log n) per get, O(1) per set - n is the number of values stored for that key
complexity.space: O(n) - all stored (timestamp, value) pairs across all keys

pitfalls: Timestamps are guaranteed strictly increasing per key by the problem, which is what makes binary search valid
Returning the exact match instead of the closest-earlier match on a miss is a common mistake

title: Find Median of Two Sorted Arrays
summary: Given two sorted arrays, find the median of the combined set of numbers in better than O(m+n) time.
approachSummary: Binary searching a partition point in the smaller array (with the corresponding partition in the other array determined by the combined left-half size) finds the split where every left element is <= every right element in O(log(min(m, n))).
walkthrough: Binary search a partition index in the smaller array. The partition in the larger array is fixed by requiring the combined left half to have (m+n+1)//2 elements. Check that the left-side maxes are <= the right-side mins on both arrays; if not, move the partition. Once balanced, the median comes from the boundary elements, accounting for odd/even total length.

complexity.time: O(log(min(m, n))) - binary search over the smaller array's partition points
complexity.space: O(1) - no extra structures beyond a few pointers

pitfalls: Binary searching over the larger array instead of the smaller one can produce negative indices in the other array
Handling the odd-total-length and even-total-length median formulas differently is easy to get backwards

title: Reverse Linked List
summary: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse it and return the new head.
approachSummary: Walking the list once while redirecting each node's next pointer to the previous node reverses the whole list in place.
walkthrough: Keep a prev pointer starting at None and a curr pointer starting at head. At each step, save curr.next, point curr.next back to prev, then advance prev to curr and curr to the saved next. When curr is None, prev is the new head.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass through the list
complexity.space: O(1) - a constant number of pointers

pitfalls: Forgetting to save next before overwriting curr.next loses the rest of the list
Returning curr instead of prev at the end returns None

title: Merge Two Linked Lists
summary: Given the heads of two sorted linked lists, merge them into one sorted linked list.
approachSummary: Repeatedly attaching the smaller of the two current nodes to a growing result list merges both sorted lists in one linear pass, mirroring the merge step of merge sort.
walkthrough: Use a dummy head to simplify edge cases. Walk both lists with two pointers; at each step attach whichever node has the smaller value to the tail of the result and advance that pointer. When one list runs out, attach the remainder of the other directly.

complexity.time: O(m + n) - one pass across both lists combined
complexity.space: O(1) - reuses existing nodes, only a dummy head and pointers

pitfalls: Forgetting to attach the leftover tail of the longer list after the other is exhausted
Advancing the tail pointer before linking it can lose track of where to attach next

title: Reorder List
summary: Given a singly linked list, reorder it in place so nodes alternate between the front and back halves.
approachSummary: Finding the middle with slow/fast pointers, reversing the second half, then weaving the two halves together node by node reorders the list without extra storage.
walkthrough: Find the middle using slow and fast pointers. Split the list there and reverse the second half in place. Merge the first half and the reversed second half by alternating nodes from each, relinking next pointers as you go.

complexity.time: O(n) - finding the middle, reversing, and merging are each linear
complexity.space: O(1) - only pointer manipulation, no extra list storage

pitfalls: Not splitting the list at the middle before reversing can create a cycle
Off-by-one errors in the slow/fast pointer split affect which half is longer
solutionCode:
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

def reorder_list(head: ListNode | None) -> None:
    if not head or not head.next:
        return
    slow, fast = head, head
    while fast.next and fast.next.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    second = slow.next
    slow.next = None
    prev = None
    while second:
        next_node = second.next
        second.next = prev
        prev = second
        second = next_node
    first, second = head, prev
    while second:
        first_next, second_next = first.next, second.next
        first.next = second
        second.next = first_next
        first, second = first_next, second_next

title: Remove Nth Node from End of List
summary: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return the head.
approachSummary: Advancing a lead pointer n steps ahead of a trail pointer, then moving both together until the lead reaches the end, positions the trail pointer just before the node to remove - in a single pass.
walkthrough: Use a dummy node before head to handle removing the head cleanly. Advance a fast pointer n+1 steps from the dummy. Then move both a slow pointer (from the dummy) and fast together until fast reaches the end. Slow now sits just before the target node - unlink it via slow.next = slow.next.next.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass with a fixed pointer gap
complexity.space: O(1) - two pointers and a dummy node

pitfalls: Not using a dummy head makes removing the actual head node an awkward special case
Advancing fast n steps instead of n+1 off-by-one's the removal target

title: Copy List with Random Pointer
summary: Given a linked list where each node has an extra pointer that can point to any node or null, create a deep copy of the list.
approachSummary: A map from original node to its copy, built in one pass, lets a second pass wire up both next and random pointers on the copies by simple lookups.
walkthrough: First pass: walk the list creating a copy of each node (value only) and store original-to-copy mappings in a hash map. Second pass: walk the original list again, and for each node set copy.next = map[node.next] and copy.random = map[node.random], using None safely for absent pointers.

complexity.time: O(n) - two linear passes
complexity.space: O(n) - the map holding one entry per node

pitfalls: Setting random pointers during the first pass can fail because the target copy might not exist yet
Forgetting that node.next or node.random can be None needs a guarded map lookup

title: Add Two Numbers
summary: Given two non-negative numbers represented as reversed linked lists of digits, add them and return the sum as a linked list.
approachSummary: Since both lists store digits least-significant-first, adding them mirrors elementary school addition: sum corresponding digits plus any carry, one node at a time.
walkthrough: Walk both lists simultaneously with a running carry, starting at 0. At each step sum the two current digits (treating a missing node as 0) plus the carry, append digit = sum % 10 to the result, and set carry = sum // 10. Continue until both lists and the carry are exhausted.

complexity.time: O(max(m, n)) - one pass across the longer list
complexity.space: O(max(m, n)) - the output list

pitfalls: Forgetting to handle a leftover carry after both lists are exhausted (e.g. 5 + 5 = 10)
Treating a missing node's value as anything other than 0

title: Linked List Cycle
summary: Given the head of a linked list, determine whether it contains a cycle.
approachSummary: A fast pointer moving two steps for every one step of a slow pointer is guaranteed to eventually lap and meet the slow pointer if and only if the list contains a cycle.
walkthrough: Start both slow and fast at head. Advance slow by one and fast by two each iteration. If fast reaches None (or fast.next is None), there's no cycle. If slow and fast ever point to the same node, a cycle exists.

complexity.time: O(n) - the fast pointer catches up within one traversal of the cycle
complexity.space: O(1) - two pointers, no extra structures

pitfalls: Using a hash set of visited nodes also works but costs O(n) space - the two-pointer trick is O(1)
Checking fast == slow before both have moved at least once gives a false positive at the start

title: Find the Duplicate Number
summary: Given an array of n+1 integers where each value is between 1 and n, find the one duplicated number without modifying the array or using extra space.
approachSummary: Treating each value as a pointer to the index it names turns the array into an implicit linked list with a cycle at the duplicate value, detectable with Floyd's algorithm without modifying the array.
walkthrough: Use slow and fast pointers both starting at index 0, moving as slow = nums[slow] and fast = nums[nums[fast]], until they meet inside the cycle. Then reset one pointer to 0 and advance both one step at a time; the node where they meet again is the duplicate value (the cycle's entrance).

complexity.time: O(n) - Floyd's cycle detection runs in linear time
complexity.space: O(1) - no extra structures, unlike a hash-set approach

pitfalls: A hash set solution is simpler but uses O(n) space, violating the problem's O(1) space constraint
Forgetting the second phase (resetting one pointer to the start) only finds a node in the cycle, not the cycle's entrance

title: LRU Cache
summary: Design a fixed-capacity cache that supports get and put in O(1) time and evicts the least recently used item when full.
approachSummary: A hash map gives O(1) key lookup, and a doubly linked list kept in usage order gives O(1) move-to-front and evict-from-back, together delivering O(1) get and put.
walkthrough: Maintain a map from key to list node, plus a doubly linked list with dummy head and tail sentinels. On get, if the key exists, unlink its node and reinsert it right after the head (most recently used), then return its value. On put, do the same reinsert-at-front for an existing key, or create and insert a new node; if capacity is exceeded, remove the node just before the tail sentinel (least recently used) and delete it from the map.

complexity.time: O(1) - both get and put are constant time
complexity.space: O(capacity) - the map and linked list together hold at most `capacity` entries

pitfalls: Using a plain OrderedDict works too but implementing the doubly linked list by hand is what the problem is testing
Forgetting to remove the evicted key from the hash map, not just the linked list, leaves a dangling reference

title: Merge K Sorted Lists
summary: Given an array of k sorted linked lists, merge them into a single sorted linked list.
approachSummary: A min-heap holding the current front node of each list always exposes the globally smallest remaining value, letting you build the merged list one pop-and-push at a time.
walkthrough: Push the head of each non-empty list into a min-heap keyed by value (with a tie-breaker index to avoid comparing nodes directly). Repeatedly pop the smallest, append it to the result, and if that node has a next, push it into the heap. Continue until the heap is empty.

complexity.time: O(n log k) - n total nodes, each heap operation costs log k for k lists
complexity.space: O(k) - the heap holds at most one node per list

pitfalls: Comparing ListNode objects directly in the heap fails without a tie-breaker since nodes aren't orderable - include an index
A pairwise-merge approach (merge lists two at a time) also works and has the same complexity, just a different structure

title: Reverse Nodes in K-Group
summary: Given a linked list, reverse the nodes of the list k at a time and return the modified list.
approachSummary: Reversing the list in fixed-size chunks of k, and reconnecting each reversed chunk to the next, handles the whole list without recursion by tracking the boundary before and after each chunk.
walkthrough: First check whether k nodes remain from the current position; if fewer than k remain, leave them as-is. Otherwise reverse exactly k nodes using the standard iterative reversal, then reconnect the chunk before it to the new head of the reversed chunk, and recurse or loop on the remainder starting from the old head (now the chunk's tail).

complexity.time: O(n) - each node is reversed exactly once
complexity.space: O(1) iterative, or O(n/k) if implemented recursively due to call stack

pitfalls: Forgetting to check that at least k nodes remain before reversing corrupts the list's tail
Losing the pointer to the node just before the group makes reconnecting the reversed chunk impossible

title: Invert Binary Tree
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, invert it so it becomes its own mirror image.
approachSummary: Swapping a node's left and right children, then recursively inverting each of those children, mirrors the entire tree in one pass.
walkthrough: If the node is None, return None. Otherwise recursively invert the left and right subtrees, then swap the node's left and right pointers to the (already inverted) results, and return the node.

complexity.time: O(n) - every node is visited once
complexity.space: O(h) - recursion stack depth equal to the tree's height

pitfalls: Swapping before recursing versus after both work, but mixing them up with partial swaps causes bugs
Forgetting the None base case causes infinite recursion or attribute errors

title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, find its maximum depth.
approachSummary: The depth of a tree is one plus the deeper of its two subtrees' depths, giving a direct recursive definition.
walkthrough: If the node is None, its depth is 0. Otherwise recursively compute the depth of the left and right subtrees, and return 1 plus the larger of the two.

complexity.time: O(n) - every node is visited once
complexity.space: O(h) - recursion stack depth equal to the tree's height

pitfalls: An iterative BFS level-count also works and avoids recursion depth limits on very unbalanced trees
Forgetting the +1 for the current node undercounts the depth

title: Diameter of a Binary Tree
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, find the length of the longest path between any two nodes.
approachSummary: The longest path through any node equals the sum of its left and right subtree heights, so tracking a running max of that sum while computing heights finds the diameter in one pass.
walkthrough: Write a height helper that recursively computes each subtree's height while, as a side effect, updating a running best diameter with left height plus right height at each node. Return the final best value after the recursion completes.

complexity.time: O(n) - a single pass computing heights bottom-up
complexity.space: O(h) - recursion stack depth equal to the tree's height

pitfalls: The diameter is measured in edges (node count minus one along the path), not always matching a naive node count
Recomputing height from scratch at every node instead of combining it with the diameter check makes this O(n^2)

title: Balanced Binary Tree
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, determine whether it is height-balanced.
approachSummary: A tree is height-balanced exactly when every node's two subtrees differ in height by at most one, which a bottom-up height computation can check while short-circuiting on the first imbalance found.
walkthrough: Write a recursive helper that returns a node's height, or a sentinel (like -1) if any subtree beneath it is already unbalanced. At each node, compute both subtree heights; if either is the imbalance sentinel or they differ by more than one, propagate the sentinel up. Otherwise return 1 plus the max height.

complexity.time: O(n) - each node is visited once with the combined height/balance check
complexity.space: O(h) - recursion stack depth equal to the tree's height

pitfalls: Computing height and checking balance as two separate top-down passes is correct but O(n^2) in the worst case
Off-by-one in the balance threshold (using > 1 versus >= 1) inverts the definition

title: Same Tree
summary: Given the roots of two binary trees, determine whether they are structurally identical with the same node values.
approachSummary: Two trees are identical exactly when their roots hold the same value and both pairs of corresponding subtrees are themselves identical, which is a direct recursive check.
walkthrough: If both nodes are None, they're equal. If exactly one is None, or their values differ, they're not equal. Otherwise recursively check that the left subtrees are equal and the right subtrees are equal.

complexity.time: O(min(m, n)) - recursion stops as soon as a mismatch is found, otherwise visits every node
complexity.space: O(h) - recursion stack depth equal to the smaller tree's height

pitfalls: Checking values before checking that both nodes are non-None causes an attribute error on a None node
Forgetting to require both subtree comparisons (left AND right) to be true

title: Subtree of Another Tree
summary: Given the roots of two binary trees, determine whether the second tree appears as a subtree of the first.
approachSummary: subRoot is a subtree of root exactly when some node in root, considered as its own tree, is identical to subRoot - checkable by combining a same-tree comparison with a tree traversal.
walkthrough: Walk every node of the main tree. At each node, check whether the subtree rooted there is identical to subRoot using a same-tree helper. If any node matches, return true. If the traversal completes without a match, return false.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - in the worst case, a same-tree check (O(n)) runs at each of m nodes
complexity.space: O(h) - recursion stack depth equal to the tree's height

pitfalls: Serializing both trees to strings and checking substring containment is a faster common trick, but needs careful delimiters to avoid false positives
An empty subRoot is trivially a subtree of any tree, including an empty one - check this edge case

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST
summary: Given a binary search tree and two of its nodes, find their lowest common ancestor.
approachSummary: In a BST, the split point where p and q's values fall on opposite sides (or one equals the current node) is exactly their lowest common ancestor, found by walking down from the root using value comparisons alone.
walkthrough: Starting at the root, compare both p.val and q.val to the current node's value. If both are less, move left. If both are greater, move right. Otherwise (they split, or one equals the current node), the current node is the LCA.

complexity.time: O(h) - walks straight down one path of the tree
complexity.space: O(1) iterative, or O(h) if implemented recursively

pitfalls: This BST-specific approach is much simpler than the general binary tree LCA algorithm - don't overcomplicate it with a generic traversal
Assuming p and q are always distinct from the root without checking the equality case

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, return the values of its nodes grouped level by level.
approachSummary: A breadth-first traversal naturally visits nodes level by level, so processing the queue one full level at a time groups values correctly.
walkthrough: Use a queue starting with the root (if not None). While the queue is non-empty, record its current size as the number of nodes in this level, pop exactly that many nodes, collect their values, and push their children for the next level. Append each level's collected values to the result.

complexity.time: O(n) - every node is visited and enqueued once
complexity.space: O(n) - the queue can hold up to the widest level's worth of nodes

pitfalls: Not snapshotting the queue's length before the inner loop causes levels to bleed into each other as children are enqueued
Using a plain list as a queue and pop(0) is O(n) per pop - use a deque instead

title: Binary Tree Right Side View
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, return the values visible when looking at the tree from the right side.
approachSummary: The rightmost node visible at each depth is exactly the last node processed at that level in a breadth-first traversal, so a level-order traversal that records only the last value per level gives the view directly.
walkthrough: Run a BFS level by level like level-order traversal, but for each level only keep the value of the last node processed (or equivalently, the first node if you enqueue right before left). Append that one value per level to the result.

complexity.time: O(n) - every node is visited once
complexity.space: O(n) - the queue can hold up to the widest level's worth of nodes

pitfalls: A DFS that prioritizes the right child first and records the first node seen at each depth also works and can be more concise
Forgetting that a level might exist entirely on the left side still needs its rightmost (only) node recorded

title: Count Good Nodes in a Binary Tree
summary: Given a binary tree, count the nodes whose value is greater than or equal to every value on the path from the root to that node.
approachSummary: A node is good exactly when it's greater than or equal to every value on the path from the root, which a DFS can check by threading the running maximum-so-far down through recursive calls.
walkthrough: Recurse with the current node and the maximum value seen on the path from the root so far. If the current node's value is >= that max, it's good - count it, and use its value as the new max going down (since it's now the largest seen). Recurse into both children with the updated max and sum the counts.

complexity.time: O(n) - every node is visited once
complexity.space: O(h) - recursion stack depth equal to the tree's height

pitfalls: Comparing against the parent's value instead of the max over the whole path so far gives the wrong answer
The root is always good by definition since its path-so-far max is itself

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, determine whether it is a valid binary search tree.
approachSummary: A node satisfies the BST property globally only if its value falls strictly within the (low, high) bounds inherited from every ancestor, not just its immediate parent - so bounds must be threaded down through recursion.
walkthrough: Recurse with a valid (low, high) range for the current node, starting at (-infinity, infinity). If the node's value isn't strictly within that range, the tree is invalid. Otherwise recurse left with the range tightened to (low, node.val) and right with (node.val, high).

complexity.time: O(n) - every node is visited once
complexity.space: O(h) - recursion stack depth equal to the tree's height

pitfalls: Only comparing a node to its immediate children/parent misses violations from a grandparent further up
An in-order traversal that checks strictly increasing values is an equally valid alternative approach

title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
summary: Given the root of a binary search tree and an integer k, find the kth smallest value in the tree.
approachSummary: An in-order traversal of a BST visits nodes in strictly increasing sorted order, so the kth value visited is the kth smallest, and the traversal can stop as soon as it's found.
walkthrough: Perform an in-order traversal (left, node, right) iteratively using an explicit stack, decrementing a counter k each time a node is visited. When the counter reaches zero, that node's value is the answer.

complexity.time: O(h + k) - descend to the leftmost node then visit k nodes
complexity.space: O(h) - the explicit stack depth equals the tree's height

pitfalls: A full recursive in-order traversal collecting all values then indexing works but wastes time and space beyond the kth element
Forgetting that in-order (not pre-order or post-order) is required for sorted output

title: Construct Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
summary: Given the preorder and inorder traversal arrays of a binary tree, reconstruct and return the tree.
approachSummary: Preorder's first element is always the current subtree's root, and that root's position in inorder splits the remaining elements into its left and right subtrees, which recursion can exploit directly.
walkthrough: Build a hash map from value to its index in inorder for O(1) lookups. Recursively take the next unused element from preorder as the root, find its index in inorder to determine the left subtree's size, then recurse to build the left subtree from the corresponding preorder/inorder slices, followed by the right subtree.

complexity.time: O(n) - each node is processed once with O(1) index lookups via the map
complexity.space: O(n) - the index map plus recursion stack

pitfalls: Using list.index() instead of a precomputed hash map makes this O(n^2)
An index pointer into preorder must be shared (not copied) across recursive calls so it advances correctly

title: Binary Tree Max Path Sum
summary: Given the root of a binary tree, find the maximum sum of any non-empty path between any two nodes.
approachSummary: The best path through any node is its value plus the best non-negative contribution from each child branch, while the value a node can contribute upward to its parent is limited to just one of those branches.
walkthrough: Recurse returning the maximum path sum extending upward from a node (its value plus the better of its two children's upward contributions, clamped at 0 to ignore negative branches). As a side effect at each node, update a global best using node.val plus both children's upward contributions, since that represents a path passing through this node.

complexity.time: O(n) - every node is visited once
complexity.space: O(h) - recursion stack depth equal to the tree's height

pitfalls: The value returned to the parent can only include one child branch, not both, since a path can't fork twice through the same node when extending upward
Forgetting to clamp negative subtree contributions at 0 lets harmful branches lower the sum

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
summary: Design an algorithm to serialize a binary tree to a string and deserialize that string back into the original tree.
approachSummary: A preorder traversal that explicitly records null markers captures enough structure to reconstruct the exact same tree shape, since preorder with nulls uniquely determines the tree without needing inorder as well.
walkthrough: To serialize, do a preorder DFS, appending each node's value to a list, or a marker like 'N' for None children, joined into a string. To deserialize, split the string back into tokens and consume them in the same preorder order: if the next token is the marker, return None; otherwise create a node, then recursively build its left and right children from the remaining tokens.

complexity.time: O(n) - each node is visited once during both serialize and deserialize
complexity.space: O(n) - the serialized string/list and the recursion stack

pitfalls: Without explicit null markers, preorder alone doesn't uniquely determine the tree shape
The deserialize helper must consume tokens from a shared iterator/pointer, not restart from the beginning each call

title: Implement Trie
summary: Implement a trie with insert, search, and startsWith operations for a set of strings.
approachSummary: Each trie node holds a map of children keyed by character plus an end-of-word flag, so inserting and searching both just walk or create nodes character by character.
walkthrough: For insert, walk the string character by character, creating a child node for any character not already present, ending by marking the final node as a complete word. For search, walk the same way but return false immediately if a character is missing, and check the end-of-word flag at the final node. For startsWith, do the same walk but skip the end-of-word check.

complexity.time: O(k) - k is the length of the word or prefix, for insert/search/startsWith each
complexity.space: O(total characters inserted) - the trie's node count in the worst case

pitfalls: Using search's logic for startsWith without skipping the end-of-word flag check gives wrong results for prefixes that aren't complete words
A plain dict of dicts works fine here in Python - no need for a fixed-size array unless the alphabet is small and known

title: Design Add and Search Word Data Structure
summary: Design a data structure that supports adding words and searching for a word, where the search may include '.' as a wildcard for any letter.
approachSummary: A trie handles addWord exactly like standard trie insertion, and search's wildcard '.' is handled by branching into every child at that position via a small DFS instead of following a single path.
walkthrough: addWord inserts characters into a trie as usual. search performs a recursive DFS over both the trie and the word: for a normal character, follow the matching child or fail if absent; for '.', try every child at that position and succeed if any recursive continuation succeeds. Success at the end of the word requires the current node to be marked as a word end.

complexity.time: O(k) for addWord; O(26^d * k) worst case for search with d wildcards
complexity.space: O(total characters inserted) - the trie's node count

pitfalls: Forgetting that '.' must try ALL children, not just the first, misses valid matches
The base case must check both that the word is exhausted AND that the current node marks a complete word

title: Word Search II
summary: Given a board of letters and a list of words, find all the words that can be formed by sequentially adjacent letters on the board.
approachSummary: Inserting all target words into a shared trie lets a single DFS over the board follow trie edges instead of restarting a separate search per word, pruning branches the moment no word matches the path so far.
walkthrough: Build a trie from all target words. DFS from every board cell, following the trie alongside the board path; if the current cell's letter isn't a trie child, stop that branch. When a trie node marks a complete word, record it (and mark it found to avoid duplicates). Mark cells visited during the DFS and unmark them on backtrack.

complexity.time: O(m * n * 4^L) - board cells times DFS branching, bounded by longest word length L, pruned heavily by the trie
complexity.space: O(total characters in words) - the trie, plus O(L) recursion depth

pitfalls: Searching each word independently with a fresh board traversal is correct but far slower than sharing one trie-guided DFS
Not marking cells as visited during the DFS (or forgetting to unmark them after) allows reusing the same cell twice in one word

title: Kth Largest Element in a Stream
summary: Design a class that finds the kth largest element in a stream of numbers as new numbers are added.
approachSummary: Keeping a min-heap of exactly the k largest values seen so far means its smallest element (the heap's root) is always the kth largest overall.
walkthrough: Initialize a min-heap with the first k elements (or fewer). On each add, push the new value, then if the heap grows beyond size k, pop the smallest. The heap's root after each add is the kth largest value.

complexity.time: O(log k) per add - a single push/pop on a size-k heap
complexity.space: O(k) - the heap holds exactly k elements

pitfalls: Using a max-heap of all elements works but costs more memory and time than bounding it to size k
Forgetting to pop when the heap exceeds size k lets it grow unbounded

title: Last Stone Weight
summary: Given the weights of stones, repeatedly smash the two heaviest together until at most one stone remains, and return its weight.
approachSummary: Repeatedly smashing the two heaviest stones is naturally expressed by a max-heap: pop the two largest, push back their difference if nonzero, and repeat until at most one stone remains.
walkthrough: Push all stone weights negated into a heap (Python's heapq is a min-heap, so negation simulates a max-heap). While more than one stone remains, pop the two largest (smallest negated), and if their difference is nonzero, push the negated difference back. Return the last stone's weight, or 0 if none remain.

complexity.time: O(n log n) - each smash is a couple of O(log n) heap operations, up to n-1 smashes
complexity.space: O(n) - the heap holds all stones

pitfalls: Forgetting to negate values both when pushing and when interpreting popped values breaks the max-heap simulation
Not handling the case where the heap empties out entirely (all stones destroyed) returns the wrong default

title: K Closest Points to Origin
summary: Given an array of points on a plane, return the k points closest to the origin.
approachSummary: Keeping a max-heap of the k closest points seen so far, evicting the farthest whenever it exceeds size k, leaves exactly the k closest points once the input is exhausted.
walkthrough: Push each point's negated squared distance (to simulate a max-heap) along with the point itself. Whenever the heap exceeds size k, pop the current farthest (largest negated distance, i.e. smallest actual negated value at top). After processing all points, the heap holds the k closest.

complexity.time: O(n log k) - each of n points does an O(log k) heap operation
complexity.space: O(k) - the heap holds at most k points

pitfalls: Using actual Euclidean distance (with a square root) is unnecessary - squared distance preserves ordering and avoids floating point
Sorting all n points by distance also works but is O(n log n), worse than the heap approach when k is small

title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
summary: Given an unsorted integer array, find the kth largest element.
approachSummary: Maintaining a min-heap capped at size k keeps exactly the k largest values seen, so its root is the kth largest once all elements are processed - or Quickselect can find it directly in expected linear time.
walkthrough: Push each value onto a min-heap, popping whenever the heap exceeds size k. After processing all values, the heap's root is the kth largest. Alternatively, Quickselect partitions around a pivot like quicksort but recurses into only the side containing the kth position, for a faster expected runtime.

complexity.time: O(n log k) with a heap, or O(n) expected with Quickselect
complexity.space: O(k) with a heap, or O(1) extra with in-place Quickselect

pitfalls: Sorting the whole array is a simple O(n log n) baseline but not optimal for large n with small k
Quickselect's worst case is O(n^2) without randomized pivot selection

title: Task Scheduler
summary: Given a list of CPU tasks and a required cooldown between identical tasks, find the minimum time needed to finish all tasks.
approachSummary: The most frequent task dictates a minimum schedule length made of (max_count - 1) full cooldown-sized groups plus the tasks tied for that max count, and idle slots only appear when there aren't enough other distinct tasks to fill the gaps.
walkthrough: Count each task's frequency. Let max_count be the highest frequency and max_count_tasks be how many tasks share that frequency. The schedule needs at least (max_count - 1) * (n + 1) + max_count_tasks slots. The answer is the larger of that formula and the total number of tasks (since with enough task variety, no idling is needed at all).

complexity.time: O(t) - t is the number of tasks, dominated by counting frequencies
complexity.space: O(1) - a fixed 26-task count array

pitfalls: A heap/queue simulation of the actual schedule also works and is more intuitive, but the closed-form math is faster
Forgetting to compare the formula's result against len(tasks) undercounts when there are enough distinct tasks to avoid idling entirely
solutionCode:
from collections import Counter

def least_interval(tasks: list[str], n: int) -> int:
    counts = Counter(tasks)
    max_count = max(counts.values())
    max_count_tasks = sum(1 for c in counts.values() if c == max_count)
    formula = (max_count - 1) * (n + 1) + max_count_tasks
    return max(formula, len(tasks))

title: Design Twitter
summary: Design a simplified version of Twitter that supports posting tweets, following/unfollowing users, and retrieving the 10 most recent tweets in a user's news feed.
approachSummary: Tagging every tweet with a global increasing timestamp and merging each followee's most recent tweets with a heap produces the 10 most recent tweets across everyone a user follows.
walkthrough: Store each user's tweets as a list of (timestamp, tweetId), using a global counter as the timestamp so ordering is consistent. For getNewsFeed, gather the most recent tweets from the user and everyone they follow, push them into a max-heap (or min-heap of negated timestamps), and pop the top 10. Maintain a follow map (set of followee ids) for follow/unfollow.

complexity.time: O(k log k) for getNewsFeed where k is tweets considered; O(1) for postTweet/follow/unfollow
complexity.space: O(u + t) - u users' follow sets plus t total tweets stored

pitfalls: Forgetting a user always sees their own tweets even without following themselves
Merging all of a followee's tweets instead of just their most recent few wastes time when a user has posted thousands of tweets

title: Find Median from Data Stream
summary: Design a data structure that supports adding numbers from a stream and finding the median of all numbers added so far.
approachSummary: Splitting numbers into a max-heap of the smaller half and a min-heap of the larger half, kept balanced in size, exposes the median at the tops of the two heaps at any time.
walkthrough: On addNum, push into the max-heap (smaller half); then move its top into the min-heap (larger half) to maintain the invariant that every value in the max-heap is <= every value in the min-heap. If the min-heap grows larger than the max-heap, move its top back. For findMedian, if the heaps are equal size, average their tops; otherwise return the max-heap's top (it holds one extra element).

complexity.time: O(log n) per addNum; O(1) for findMedian
complexity.space: O(n) - the two heaps together hold all numbers seen

pitfalls: Python's heapq is min-heap only, so the 'smaller half' heap must store negated values to act as a max-heap
Letting the two heaps drift more than one element apart in size breaks the median formula

title: Subsets
summary: Given an array of unique integers, return all possible subsets.
approachSummary: Every subset corresponds to a sequence of include/exclude decisions for each element, so recursively deciding to add or skip each element in turn enumerates all subsets.
walkthrough: Recurse over elements by index, maintaining a current partial subset. At each index, record the current subset as one result, then recurse including the element at that index, backtrack (remove it), and recurse without it (implicitly, by just moving to the next index) - or equivalently branch into include/exclude at each step.

complexity.time: O(n * 2^n) - 2^n subsets, each taking O(n) to copy into the result
complexity.space: O(n) - recursion depth plus the current subset being built

pitfalls: Appending the same mutable list reference to the result instead of a copy causes every result to reflect later mutations
Forgetting to backtrack (pop) after the recursive include branch corrupts subsequent subsets
solutionCode:
def subsets(nums: list[int]) -> list[list[int]]:
    result: list[list[int]] = []
    current: list[int] = []

    def backtrack(start: int) -> None:
        result.append(current[:])
        for i in range(start, len(nums)):
            current.append(nums[i])
            backtrack(i + 1)
            current.pop()

    backtrack(0)
    return result

title: Combination Sum
summary: Given an array of distinct candidate numbers and a target, return all unique combinations where the candidates sum to the target, allowing unlimited reuse of each candidate.
approachSummary: Since a candidate can be reused unlimited times, exploring 'take this candidate again or move to the next candidate' at each step, while tracking the remaining target, finds all combinations that sum exactly to it.
walkthrough: Recurse with a start index and a remaining target. If remaining hits zero, record the current combination. If remaining goes negative, stop. Otherwise, for each candidate from the start index onward, add it to the current combination, recurse with the same index (allowing reuse) and reduced target, then backtrack.

complexity.time: O(n^(t/m)) - roughly bounded by target t divided by the smallest candidate m, in the branching factor n
complexity.space: O(t/m) - recursion depth bounded by how many times the smallest candidate can be used

pitfalls: Recursing with index i (not i + 1) is what allows reusing the same candidate multiple times - using i + 1 would forbid reuse
Not sorting candidates first misses an easy pruning opportunity when remaining goes negative

title: Permutations
summary: Given an array of distinct integers, return all possible permutations.
approachSummary: Building a permutation one position at a time, choosing from whatever elements haven't been used yet, and backtracking after each choice enumerates every ordering.
walkthrough: Recurse with a current partial permutation and a set (or boolean array) of used indices. If the permutation is full length, record it. Otherwise try each unused element, mark it used, append it, recurse, then unmark and remove it (backtrack).

complexity.time: O(n * n!) - n! permutations, each taking O(n) to copy into the result
complexity.space: O(n) - recursion depth plus the used-tracking structure

pitfalls: Swapping elements in place is a faster alternative to a used-set but is easier to get wrong with the swap-back step
Forgetting to copy the current permutation when appending to results shares a mutable reference
solutionCode:
def permute(nums: list[int]) -> list[list[int]]:
    result: list[list[int]] = []
    current: list[int] = []
    used = [False] * len(nums)

    def backtrack() -> None:
        if len(current) == len(nums):
            result.append(current[:])
            return
        for i, n in enumerate(nums):
            if used[i]:
                continue
            used[i] = True
            current.append(n)
            backtrack()
            current.pop()
            used[i] = False

    backtrack()
    return result

title: Subsets II
summary: Given an integer array that may contain duplicates, return all possible subsets without duplicate subsets.
approachSummary: Sorting the array first groups duplicate values together, so skipping a duplicate value at the same recursion depth (rather than globally) avoids generating the same subset twice while still allowing duplicates within a single subset.
walkthrough: Sort nums so duplicates are adjacent. Recurse like standard subsets, but within the loop at each recursion level, skip an index if it holds the same value as the previous index AND the previous index was already considered (not taken) at this same level - this prevents duplicate subsets without banning legitimate reuse of a value across different subsets.

complexity.time: O(n * 2^n) - same asymptotic bound as subsets, with sorting adding O(n log n)
complexity.space: O(n) - recursion depth plus the current subset

pitfalls: The duplicate-skip condition compares to the previous sibling at the same recursion level, not the previous element overall
Forgetting to sort first makes the adjacent-duplicate skip logic invalid
solutionCode:
def subsets_with_dup(nums: list[int]) -> list[list[int]]:
    nums.sort()
    result: list[list[int]] = []
    current: list[int] = []

    def backtrack(start: int) -> None:
        result.append(current[:])
        for i in range(start, len(nums)):
            if i > start and nums[i] == nums[i - 1]:
                continue
            current.append(nums[i])
            backtrack(i + 1)
            current.pop()

    backtrack(0)
    return result

title: Combination Sum II
summary: Given a collection of candidate numbers that may contain duplicates and a target, find all unique combinations that sum to the target, using each number at most once.
approachSummary: Sorting first and, at each recursion level, skipping a candidate that equals the previous sibling avoids duplicate combinations, while advancing the index by one (rather than staying) enforces that each candidate is used at most once.
walkthrough: Sort the candidates. Recurse with a start index and remaining target, adding the current combination when remaining hits zero. In the loop, skip a candidate equal to the previous one already tried at this level. Recurse with index i + 1 (not i) since each candidate can only be used once, then backtrack.

complexity.time: O(2^n) - bounded by the number of subsets considered, pruned by sorting and duplicate skipping
complexity.space: O(n) - recursion depth plus the current combination

pitfalls: Using index i instead of i + 1 in the recursive call would incorrectly allow reusing the same element multiple times
Sorting is required both to enable early termination on remaining < 0 and to make the duplicate-skip logic valid

title: Word Search
summary: Given a grid of letters and a word, determine whether the word can be formed by sequentially adjacent letters on the grid.
approachSummary: Starting a DFS from every board cell that matches the word's first letter, and following adjacent cells that match subsequent letters while marking visited cells, finds any valid path spelling the word.
walkthrough: For each cell matching word[0], start a DFS: if the current position matches the current word index and all previous checks passed, mark the cell visited (e.g. overwrite it temporarily), recurse into all four neighbors for the next letter, then unmark it (backtrack) regardless of the outcome. Succeed once the word index reaches the word's length.

complexity.time: O(m * n * 4^L) - board cells times DFS branching, bounded by word length L
complexity.space: O(L) - recursion depth equal to the word length

pitfalls: Forgetting to unmark (restore) a visited cell after backtracking corrupts later search paths
