Not checking board bounds before indexing into neighbors causes an index error

title: Palindrome Partitioning
summary: Given a string, partition it so every substring of the partition is a palindrome, and return all possible partitions.
approachSummary: Choosing every possible palindromic prefix at each position and recursing on the remainder enumerates all ways to partition the string into palindromic substrings.
walkthrough: Recurse with a start index. Try every end index from start+1 to the string's length; if the substring from start to that end is a palindrome, add it to the current partition, recurse from that end, then backtrack. Record the current partition as a full result once start reaches the string's length.

complexity.time: O(n * 2^n) - up to 2^n partitions, each palindrome check up to O(n)
complexity.space: O(n) - recursion depth plus the current partition

pitfalls: Checking palindrome-ness with string slicing is O(n) each time - precomputing a palindrome table can speed this up for large inputs
Forgetting to backtrack after adding a substring to the current partition

title: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
summary: Given a string of digits from 2-9, return all possible letter combinations that the number could represent on a phone keypad.
approachSummary: Each digit maps to a fixed set of letters, so recursively choosing one letter per digit position and moving to the next digit enumerates every combination.
walkthrough: Map each digit to its letters. Recurse with an index into the digit string and a partial combination. If the index reaches the string's length, record the combination. Otherwise, for each letter mapped to the current digit, append it, recurse on the next index, then backtrack.

complexity.time: O(4^n * n) - up to 4 letters per digit (for digits 7 and 9), n digits, each combination costs O(n) to build
complexity.space: O(n) - recursion depth equal to the number of digits

pitfalls: An empty input string should return an empty list, not a list containing an empty string
Digits 0 and 1 have no letters mapped and typically aren't part of valid input

title: N-Queens
summary: Given an integer n, place n queens on an n x n chessboard so no two queens attack each other, and return all distinct solutions.
approachSummary: Placing one queen per row and tracking which columns and diagonals are already threatened lets backtracking prune invalid placements immediately instead of checking the whole board each time.
walkthrough: Recurse row by row, trying each column for the current row's queen. Track occupied columns and both diagonal directions (row - col and row + col are constant along each diagonal) with sets. If a column/diagonal is free, place the queen, recurse to the next row, then remove it (backtrack) and clear its column/diagonal marks. Record a full board once all rows are placed.

complexity.time: O(n!) - roughly, pruned heavily by the column/diagonal constraints
complexity.space: O(n^2) - the board representation, plus O(n) for the constraint sets

pitfalls: Checking all previously placed queens for conflicts each time is correct but far slower than O(1) set lookups for columns and diagonals
The two diagonal directions need two different invariants: row - col for one, row + col for the other
solutionCode:
def solve_n_queens(n: int) -> list[list[str]]:
    result: list[list[str]] = []
    cols: set[int] = set()
    diag1: set[int] = set()
    diag2: set[int] = set()
    placement: list[int] = []

    def backtrack(row: int) -> None:
        if row == n:
            board = []
            for c in placement:
                board.append('.' * c + 'Q' + '.' * (n - c - 1))
            result.append(board)
            return
        for col in range(n):
            if col in cols or (row - col) in diag1 or (row + col) in diag2:
                continue
            cols.add(col)
            diag1.add(row - col)
            diag2.add(row + col)
            placement.append(col)
            backtrack(row + 1)
            placement.pop()
            cols.remove(col)
            diag1.remove(row - col)
            diag2.remove(row + col)

    backtrack(0)
    return result

title: Number of Islands
summary: Given a 2D grid of '1's (land) and '0's (water), count the number of islands.
approachSummary: Each unvisited land cell found while scanning the grid is the start of a new island, and flood-filling all connected land from it marks the whole island visited so it's never recounted.
walkthrough: Scan every cell. Whenever an unvisited '1' is found, increment the island count and run a DFS or BFS flood fill from it, marking every connected land cell (via the four cardinal directions) as visited so the scan skips them later.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every cell is visited a constant number of times total
complexity.space: O(m * n) - worst case recursion/queue depth if the whole grid is one island

pitfalls: Modifying the grid in place (e.g. setting visited cells to '0') avoids a separate visited set but mutates the input - use a visited set if that's undesirable
Forgetting to bound-check neighbors before indexing causes an out-of-range error

title: Clone Graph
summary: Given a reference to a node in a connected undirected graph, return a deep copy of the graph.
approachSummary: A map from original node to its clone, built during a DFS, lets each node's clone be created once and referenced consistently even when the graph has cycles.
walkthrough: DFS from the given node. If a node has already been cloned (present in the map), return its clone directly to avoid infinite recursion on cycles. Otherwise create a clone, store it in the map immediately (before recursing on neighbors), then recursively clone each neighbor and append the results to the clone's neighbor list.

complexity.time: O(v + e) - every node and edge is visited once
complexity.space: O(v) - the map holding one clone per original node

pitfalls: Storing the new clone in the map before recursing into neighbors is essential to correctly handle cycles - otherwise infinite recursion results
Forgetting the empty-graph edge case (a None input node)

title: Max Area of Island
summary: Given a 2D grid of 0's and 1's, find the area of the largest island of connected 1's.
approachSummary: Flood-filling each unvisited island while counting the cells visited gives that island's area directly, and tracking the largest area seen across all islands answers the question.
walkthrough: Scan every cell. On an unvisited land cell, run a DFS that marks cells visited and returns 1 plus the sum of areas from its four neighbors (recursively). Track the maximum area returned across all islands found during the scan.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every cell is visited a constant number of times total
complexity.space: O(m * n) - worst case recursion depth if the whole grid is one island

pitfalls: The area DFS must return a count, not just mark visited, unlike the plain 'count islands' variant of this problem
Marking visited cells in place (setting to 0) avoids a separate visited set but mutates the input grid

title: Pacific Atlantic Waterflow
summary: Given a grid of heights bordered by the Pacific and Atlantic oceans, find every cell from which water can flow to both oceans.
approachSummary: Instead of checking, for every cell, whether water can flow forward to each ocean, it's far cheaper to flow backward from each ocean's border cells uphill, marking reachable cells, and intersect the two reachable sets.
walkthrough: Run a DFS/BFS from every Pacific-adjacent border cell (top row and left column), moving to neighbors with height >= current height (reversed flow), marking reachable cells. Do the same from every Atlantic-adjacent border cell (bottom row and right column). The answer is every cell reachable from both.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - each ocean's flood fill visits each cell a constant number of times
complexity.space: O(m * n) - two visited sets, one per ocean

pitfalls: Flowing forward from every single cell to check both oceans is correct but O((m*n)^2) in the worst case - reversing the flow from the ocean borders is the key optimization
The reversed-flow condition is neighbor height >= current height, not <=, since water actually flows downhill

title: Surrounded Regions
summary: Given a board of 'X' and 'O', flip every 'O' that is completely surrounded by 'X' into 'X'.
approachSummary: A region of 'O's is only safe from being flipped if it connects to the border, so flood-filling from every border 'O' first identifies the safe cells, letting every other 'O' be flipped to 'X'.
walkthrough: Run a DFS/BFS from every 'O' on the grid's border, marking all connected 'O's as safe (e.g. temporarily marked with a placeholder character). After that pass, walk the whole grid: flip any remaining unmarked 'O' to 'X' (it was surrounded), and restore the placeholder marks back to 'O'.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - the border flood fill and the final grid pass are both linear
complexity.space: O(m * n) - worst case recursion depth if most of the grid is connected 'O's

pitfalls: Flipping every non-border-connected 'O' during the initial flood fill accidentally flips safe cells not yet visited - use a placeholder and restore afterward
Forgetting that a region needs to touch the border on any side, not just the top-left corner

title: Rotting Oranges
summary: Given a grid of fresh, rotten, and empty cells, find the minimum minutes until no fresh orange remains, as rot spreads to adjacent cells each minute.
approachSummary: Starting a BFS simultaneously from every already-rotten orange and expanding one minute (one BFS layer) at a time models how rot actually spreads in lockstep across all sources.
walkthrough: Enqueue all initially rotten oranges along with a fresh count of oranges. Run BFS layer by layer, where each layer represents one minute: for every rotten orange in the current layer, rot any fresh neighbor, decrement the fresh count, and enqueue it for the next layer. Track minutes elapsed. If fresh oranges remain after the BFS ends, return -1; otherwise return the elapsed minutes.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every cell is enqueued and processed at most once
complexity.space: O(m * n) - the queue can hold up to all cells in the worst case

pitfalls: Running a separate BFS from each rotten orange one at a time gives the wrong minute count - all sources must expand together, layer by layer
Forgetting to check for leftover fresh oranges after the BFS misses unreachable oranges

title: Walls and Gates
summary: Given a grid of rooms, walls, and gates, fill each empty room with the distance to its nearest gate.
approachSummary: Running a BFS simultaneously from every gate and expanding outward naturally assigns each empty room the shortest distance to its nearest gate, since BFS explores in increasing distance order.
walkthrough: Enqueue every gate (cell with value 0). Run BFS layer by layer; for each cell popped, look at its four neighbors, and if a neighbor is an empty room (INF), set its distance to the current distance + 1 and enqueue it. Walls are never enqueued and act as natural barriers.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every cell is enqueued and processed at most once
complexity.space: O(m * n) - the queue can hold up to all gate/room cells

pitfalls: Running BFS from each room to find its nearest gate individually is correct but far slower - starting from all gates at once is the standard multi-source trick
Forgetting to skip walls (-1) when checking neighbors lets rooms be reached through them

title: Course Schedule
summary: Given a number of courses and a list of prerequisite pairs, determine whether it's possible to finish all courses.
approachSummary: All courses can be finished exactly when the prerequisite graph has no cycle, which can be checked either by Kahn's BFS topological sort (all nodes get processed) or by DFS coloring (no back-edge into a node still on the current path).
walkthrough: Build an adjacency list of prerequisites and an in-degree count per course. Use Kahn's algorithm: enqueue all courses with in-degree 0, and repeatedly pop a course, decrement its neighbors' in-degrees, enqueueing any that hit 0. If the number of courses processed equals the total, there's no cycle and all courses can be finished.

complexity.time: O(v + e) - courses plus prerequisite edges, visited once each
complexity.space: O(v + e) - the adjacency list and in-degree array

pitfalls: A DFS-based cycle detection with a 'currently visiting' set works equally well and is a common alternative
Forgetting that a course can have zero prerequisites and starts directly enqueued
solutionCode:
from collections import deque

def can_finish(num_courses: int, prerequisites: list[list[int]]) -> bool:
    graph: list[list[int]] = [[] for _ in range(num_courses)]
    in_degree = [0] * num_courses
    for course, prereq in prerequisites:
        graph[prereq].append(course)
        in_degree[course] += 1
    queue = deque(c for c in range(num_courses) if in_degree[c] == 0)
    finished = 0
    while queue:
        course = queue.popleft()
        finished += 1
        for nxt in graph[course]:
            in_degree[nxt] -= 1
            if in_degree[nxt] == 0:
                queue.append(nxt)
    return finished == num_courses

title: Course Schedule II
summary: Given a number of courses and prerequisite pairs, return a valid order to take all the courses, or an empty array if impossible.
approachSummary: Kahn's algorithm not only detects a cycle but, by the order courses are dequeued, directly produces a valid topological ordering satisfying all prerequisites.
walkthrough: Same setup as Course Schedule: build an adjacency list and in-degree counts, enqueue all in-degree-0 courses. Each time a course is dequeued, append it to the result order and decrement its neighbors' in-degrees, enqueueing newly zeroed ones. If the result contains all courses at the end, return it; otherwise return an empty array (a cycle exists).

complexity.time: O(v + e) - courses plus prerequisite edges, visited once each
complexity.space: O(v + e) - the adjacency list, in-degree array, and result order

pitfalls: Returning the order built so far even when a cycle exists is wrong - the problem expects an empty array when it's impossible
The dequeue order IS a valid topological order - no additional sorting is needed
solutionCode:
from collections import deque

def find_order(num_courses: int, prerequisites: list[list[int]]) -> list[int]:
    graph: list[list[int]] = [[] for _ in range(num_courses)]
    in_degree = [0] * num_courses
    for course, prereq in prerequisites:
        graph[prereq].append(course)
        in_degree[course] += 1
    queue = deque(c for c in range(num_courses) if in_degree[c] == 0)
    order = []
    while queue:
        course = queue.popleft()
        order.append(course)
        for nxt in graph[course]:
            in_degree[nxt] -= 1
            if in_degree[nxt] == 0:
                queue.append(nxt)
    return order if len(order) == num_courses else []

title: Redundant Connection
summary: Given a graph that started as a tree and had one extra edge added, find the edge that can be removed to restore a tree.
approachSummary: Adding edges one at a time with union-find, the first edge that connects two nodes already in the same component is exactly the redundant edge that created the cycle.
walkthrough: Initialize a union-find (disjoint set) structure over all nodes. Process edges in order; for each edge, find the roots of its two endpoints. If they're already the same root, this edge is redundant - return it. Otherwise union the two components and continue.

complexity.time: O(n * alpha(n)) - near-constant per union-find operation with path compression and union by rank
complexity.space: O(n) - the parent and rank arrays

pitfalls: A DFS-based cycle check per edge also works but is O(n) per edge, making it O(n^2) overall versus union-find's near-linear time
Forgetting path compression or union by rank degrades union-find to O(n) per operation in the worst case

title: Number of Connected Components in Graph
summary: Given n nodes and a list of undirected edges, find the number of connected components in the graph.
approachSummary: Union-find naturally counts connected components: starting with n separate components, each successful union of two previously-separate nodes reduces the component count by one.
walkthrough: Initialize union-find with n components. For each edge, union its two endpoints; if they had different roots (a real merge happened), decrement a running component count starting at n. After processing all edges, the running count is the answer.

complexity.time: O(n + e * alpha(n)) - near-linear with path compression and union by rank
complexity.space: O(n) - the parent and rank arrays

pitfalls: A DFS/BFS flood fill counting separate traversals also works and is equally valid for this problem
Only decrement the component count on an actual merge (different roots), not on every edge processed

title: Graph Valid Tree
summary: Given n nodes and a list of undirected edges, determine whether they form a valid tree.
approachSummary: A graph with n nodes is a valid tree exactly when it's fully connected AND has exactly n-1 edges - too few edges means it's disconnected, and a repeated union (cycle) means too many.
walkthrough: First check that the edge count is exactly n - 1; if not, it can't be a tree. Then use union-find to process every edge: if any edge connects two nodes already in the same component, a cycle exists and it's not a tree. If all edges union successfully with no cycle and the edge count was right, it's a valid tree.

complexity.time: O(n * alpha(n)) - near-linear with path compression and union by rank
complexity.space: O(n) - the parent and rank arrays

pitfalls: Checking only for cycles without also checking the edge count misses disconnected graphs with too few edges
n - 1 edges with no cycle guarantees full connectivity for n nodes, which is why both checks together suffice

title: Word Ladder
summary: Given a start word, an end word, and a word list, find the length of the shortest transformation sequence changing one letter at a time, where each intermediate word is in the list.
approachSummary: Treating each word as a graph node connected to every word reachable by changing one letter turns finding the shortest transformation sequence into an unweighted shortest-path BFS.
walkthrough: Put all words in a set for O(1) lookup. BFS from beginWord, tracking the transformation length. At each word, try changing every position to every other letter of the alphabet; if the resulting word is in the word set, enqueue it with length + 1 and remove it from the set (to avoid revisiting). Return the length when endWord is reached, or 0 if the BFS exhausts without finding it.

complexity.time: O(n * L^2) - n words, L letters per word, trying 26 substitutions per position
complexity.space: O(n) - the word set and BFS queue

pitfalls: Generating all neighbors by comparing against every other word in the list (checking Hamming distance 1) is a valid but slower alternative to generating candidates via letter substitution
Forgetting to remove visited words from the set causes redundant requeuing and can loop

title: Reconstruct Itinerary
summary: Given a list of airline tickets as [from, to] pairs, reconstruct the itinerary in order starting from 'JFK' that uses every ticket exactly once.
approachSummary: Finding an itinerary using every ticket exactly once is an Eulerian path problem, solvable by greedily following the lexicographically smallest unused destination and backtracking (post-order) whenever a dead end is hit, which produces the route in reverse.
walkthrough: Build an adjacency map from airport to a sorted (or min-heap) list of destinations. DFS from 'JFK', always visiting the lexicographically smallest unused destination first by removing it from the list as it's used. When a node has no more outgoing tickets, append it to the route (post-order). Reverse the final route to get the itinerary in visiting order.

complexity.time: O(e log e) - e tickets, sorting or heap operations per edge
complexity.space: O(e) - the adjacency map holding all tickets

pitfalls: A straightforward greedy without backtracking fails on itineraries that require revisiting a 'used up' path later - Hierholzer's post-order construction handles this correctly
Removing a destination from the adjacency list as it's used (rather than a separate visited set) is what allows using each ticket exactly once, including duplicate tickets

title: Min Cost to Connect all Points
summary: Given the coordinates of points on a plane, find the minimum total cost to connect all points, where the cost between two points is their Manhattan distance.
approachSummary: Connecting all points with minimum total edge cost is a minimum spanning tree problem, solved efficiently by Prim's algorithm: repeatedly add the cheapest edge that connects a new point to the already-connected set.
walkthrough: Start with one point in the MST and a min-heap of (cost, point) edges to all other points. Repeatedly pop the cheapest edge; if it leads to an already-included point, skip it. Otherwise add that point to the MST, add its cost to the total, and push edges from the newly added point to all points not yet included.

complexity.time: O(n^2 log n) - n points, each potentially pushing up to n edges onto the heap
complexity.space: O(n^2) - the heap can hold up to O(n^2) edges in the worst case

pitfalls: Kruskal's algorithm with union-find is an equally valid alternative, often simpler when edges are pre-sorted
Manhattan distance (not Euclidean) is the edge weight for this specific problem - using the wrong distance metric gives wrong costs
solutionCode:
import heapq

def min_cost_connect_points(points: list[list[int]]) -> int:
    n = len(points)
    visited = [False] * n
    heap = [(0, 0)]
    total = 0
    count = 0
    while count < n:
        cost, i = heapq.heappop(heap)
        if visited[i]:
            continue
        visited[i] = True
        total += cost
        count += 1
        for j in range(n):
            if not visited[j]:
                dist = abs(points[i][0] - points[j][0]) + abs(points[i][1] - points[j][1])
                heapq.heappush(heap, (dist, j))
    return total

title: Network Delay Time
summary: Given a network of nodes and weighted directed edges, find the time it takes for a signal sent from a source node to reach every other node.
approachSummary: The time for a signal to reach every node is the longest of the shortest paths from the source, which Dijkstra's algorithm computes directly using a min-heap to always expand the currently-closest unvisited node.
walkthrough: Build a weighted adjacency list. Run Dijkstra's from the source using a min-heap of (distance, node), maintaining a distances map. Pop the closest unvisited node, finalize its distance, and relax its outgoing edges by pushing improved distances onto the heap. After the algorithm completes, the answer is the maximum finalized distance, or -1 if any node was unreachable.

complexity.time: O(e log v) - standard Dijkstra's with a binary heap
complexity.space: O(v + e) - the adjacency list, distances map, and heap

pitfalls: Bellman-Ford also works and handles negative weights, but Dijkstra's is faster here since all weights are positive
Forgetting to check that every node was reached before returning the max distance misses the unreachable-node case

title: Swim in Rising Water
summary: Given an n x n grid of elevations, find the minimum time needed to swim from the top-left to the bottom-right as the water level rises.
approachSummary: The time to reach the destination is determined by the highest elevation you're forced to cross on the best path, which is exactly what a Dijkstra's variant minimizes when the 'distance' to a cell is defined as the max elevation along the path, not the sum.
walkthrough: Use a min-heap of (max elevation so far, row, col) starting at the origin. Pop the cell with the smallest max-elevation path; if it's the destination, return that value. Otherwise for each unvisited neighbor, push (max(current max, neighbor's elevation), neighbor) onto the heap and mark it visited.

complexity.time: O(n^2 log n) - grid cells, each with a heap operation
complexity.space: O(n^2) - the visited set and heap

pitfalls: This is Dijkstra's algorithm with a max-based cost function instead of a sum-based one - relaxation logic must reflect that
A binary search on the answer combined with BFS/DFS flood-fill at each candidate time is an equally valid alternative approach

title: Alien Dictionary
summary: Given a list of words sorted according to an unknown alien alphabet's rules, derive a valid ordering of that alphabet's letters.
approachSummary: Comparing each pair of adjacent words in the sorted word list reveals one ordering constraint between two letters (the first place they differ), and topologically sorting all such constraints reconstructs the alien alphabet's order.
walkthrough: Initialize a graph with every unique letter as a node. For each pair of adjacent words, find the first differing character and add a directed edge from the earlier word's letter to the later word's letter; if no difference is found but the earlier word is longer, the order is invalid. Topologically sort the resulting graph (via Kahn's algorithm or DFS); a cycle means no valid order exists.

complexity.time: O(c) - c is the total length of all words, for building constraints, plus O(v + e) for the topological sort
complexity.space: O(1) or O(v + e) - bounded by the alphabet size (at most 26) plus edges

pitfalls: A word that is a prefix of an earlier word (e.g. 'abc' before 'ab') is always invalid and must be checked explicitly
Only the FIRST differing character between adjacent words gives a valid constraint - comparing all characters can introduce wrong edges

title: Cheapest Flights with K Stops
summary: Given flights with prices between cities, find the cheapest price from a source to a destination using at most k stops.
approachSummary: Because the number of allowed stops caps the number of edges usable in the path, a Bellman-Ford relaxation run for exactly k+1 rounds finds the cheapest path using at most k+1 edges, which plain Dijkstra's doesn't naturally bound.
walkthrough: Initialize distances with the source at 0 and everything else infinity. Repeat k+1 times: take a snapshot of the current distances, and for every edge (u, v, cost), if distances[u] (from the snapshot) plus cost improves v's distance, update it. Using a snapshot each round ensures each round only adds one more edge/stop to any path.

complexity.time: O(k * e) - k+1 rounds, each relaxing all e edges
complexity.space: O(v) - the distances array (times two for the snapshot)

pitfalls: Dijkstra's algorithm doesn't respect the stop-count limit naturally since it always expands the globally cheapest node next, not bounded by hops
Updating distances in place during a round (instead of from a snapshot) lets a single round chain multiple edges together, violating the stop limit

title: Climbing Stairs
summary: Given a staircase of n steps where you can climb 1 or 2 steps at a time, find how many distinct ways there are to reach the top.
approachSummary: The number of ways to reach step n is the sum of the ways to reach step n-1 (then take one step) and step n-2 (then take two steps), which is exactly the Fibonacci recurrence.
walkthrough: Track only the previous two values instead of a full array. Starting from ways(1) = 1 and ways(2) = 2, iterate up to n, at each step setting the new value to the sum of the previous two and shifting the window forward.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass computing each step once
complexity.space: O(1) - only the last two values are kept

pitfalls: A naive recursive solution without memoization is exponential - always cache or iterate bottom-up
Off-by-one errors on the base cases for n = 1 and n = 2 are common

title: Min Cost Climbing Stairs
summary: Given the cost of each step on a staircase, find the minimum cost to reach the top, starting from step 0 or 1 and moving 1 or 2 steps at a time.
approachSummary: The cheapest way to reach any step is the cost of that step plus the cheaper of the two ways to have reached one or two steps before it, giving a simple bottom-up recurrence.
walkthrough: Track the minimum cost to reach the previous two steps, starting both at 0 (since you can start at index 0 or 1 for free). Iterate through the steps, at each one computing cost[i] + min(prev two), and shift the window forward. The answer is the minimum of the final two values (reaching past the last step).

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass computing each step's cost once
complexity.space: O(1) - only the last two values are kept

pitfalls: The 'top' is one step past the last stair, so the answer needs min of the last two computed costs, not just the very last one
Starting the DP array at cost 0 for the first two indices (free starting points) rather than cost[0]/cost[1] is a common off-by-one

title: House Robber
summary: Given the amount of money stashed in a row of houses, find the maximum amount you can rob without robbing two adjacent houses.
approachSummary: At each house, the best haul is either skipping it (keeping the best haul up to the previous house) or robbing it (its value plus the best haul up to two houses back), since adjacent houses can't both be robbed.
walkthrough: Track the best totals ending at the previous two houses. For each house, compute the new best as max(skip this house = prev best, rob this house = house value + best from two back), and shift the window forward. The final value is the answer.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over the houses
complexity.space: O(1) - only the last two running totals are kept

pitfalls: A full DP array works too but isn't necessary since only the last two values are ever needed
Forgetting that robbing a house adds to the total from two houses back, not one, since adjacent houses are forbidden

title: House Robber II
summary: Given the amount of money stashed in houses arranged in a circle, find the maximum amount you can rob without robbing two adjacent houses.
approachSummary: Since the houses form a circle, the first and last houses can't both be robbed, so the answer is the better of two linear House Robber runs: one excluding the last house, one excluding the first.
walkthrough: Run the standard House Robber DP twice: once on all houses except the last, and once on all houses except the first. Return the maximum of the two results. Handle the single-house edge case separately since excluding either end would leave an empty list.

complexity.time: O(n) - two linear passes over the houses
complexity.space: O(1) - only a constant number of running totals per pass

pitfalls: Running the linear House Robber DP on the full circular array without excluding an endpoint ignores the adjacency wraparound
The single-house case must be handled separately since both sub-arrays would otherwise be empty

title: Longest Palindroming Substring
summary: Given a string, find the longest substring that is a palindrome.
approachSummary: Every palindrome has a center (a single character for odd length, or a gap between two characters for even length), so expanding outward from each of the 2n-1 possible centers while both sides match finds every palindrome directly.
walkthrough: For each index, expand around it twice: once treating it as an odd-length center, once treating the gap after it as an even-length center. While the characters on both sides match and stay in bounds, keep expanding and track the longest palindrome found so far.

complexity.time: O(n^2) - n centers, each expansion up to O(n) in the worst case
complexity.space: O(1) - only index variables and the best answer tracked, no extra structures

pitfalls: Forgetting to check both odd-length and even-length centers misses palindromes like 'abba'
A full O(n^2) DP table approach also works but uses O(n^2) space versus this approach's O(1)

title: Palindrome Substrings
summary: Given a string, count how many substrings of it are palindromes.
approachSummary: Expanding around each of the 2n-1 possible centers and counting every successful expansion tallies every palindromic substring, since each one corresponds to exactly one center and expansion length.
walkthrough: For each index, expand around it as both an odd-length center and an even-length center (the gap after it). Each time the characters on both sides match within bounds, increment a running count - that expansion represents one valid palindromic substring. Continue expanding until a mismatch or the bounds are exceeded.

complexity.time: O(n^2) - n centers, each expansion up to O(n) in the worst case
complexity.space: O(1) - only a running counter and index variables

pitfalls: Counting only odd-length centers misses even-length palindromes entirely
Every single character is itself a palindrome of length 1 - make sure the count includes these

title: Decode Ways
summary: Given a string of digits where 'A' to 'Z' map to '1' to '26', find how many ways the string can be decoded into letters.
approachSummary: The number of ways to decode a prefix of the string is the sum of the ways to decode it treating the last one digit as a letter (if valid) and the last two digits as a letter (if valid), a direct bottom-up recurrence.
walkthrough: Track the number of ways to decode up to the previous two positions, starting with an empty-string base case of 1. At each position, add the ways from one digit back if the current single digit is nonzero (1-9), and add the ways from two digits back if the last two digits form a valid letter code (10-26).

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass through the string
complexity.space: O(1) - only the last two running totals are kept

pitfalls: A leading or standalone '0' is never valid as a single digit, since letters are coded 1-26, not 0-26
Forgetting to check the two-digit code is between 10 and 26 (not just <= 26) allows invalid codes like '01'

title: Coin Change
summary: Given coin denominations and a target amount, find the fewest number of coins needed to make that amount, or -1 if it's impossible.
approachSummary: The fewest coins to make amount a is one plus the fewest coins to make a - coin, minimized over every coin denomination, since a coin can be used any number of times.
walkthrough: Build a DP array indexed by amount from 0 to the target, initialized to infinity except dp[0] = 0. For each amount, try every coin; if the coin's value doesn't exceed the amount, consider dp[amount - coin] + 1 as a candidate and keep the minimum. The answer is dp[target], or -1 if it's still infinity.

complexity.time: O(amount * coins) - each amount considers every coin denomination
complexity.space: O(amount) - the DP array

pitfalls: A greedy largest-coin-first approach fails for coin systems that aren't canonical (e.g. coins [1, 3, 4] for amount 6)
Forgetting to check dp[amount] is still infinity at the end returns a wrong finite number instead of -1

title: Maximum Product Subarray
summary: Given an integer array, find the contiguous subarray with the largest product.
approachSummary: Because a negative number can flip the sign of a running product, tracking both the running maximum and running minimum ending at each position (since a very negative min times a new negative can become the new max) captures the best subarray product.
walkthrough: Track both a running max and running min ending at the current index. At each new number, compute the three candidates: the number alone, max * number, and min * number - the new max and min are the largest and smallest of these three. Update a global best with the new max at each step.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over the array
complexity.space: O(1) - only running max, min, and best values

pitfalls: Tracking only a running maximum (like Kadane's for sums) misses cases where a large negative min becomes the best max after multiplying by another negative
A zero in the array resets both running max and min to effectively restart the subarray from the next position
solutionCode:
def max_product(nums: list[int]) -> int:
    result = curr_max = curr_min = nums[0]
    for num in nums[1:]:
        candidates = (num, curr_max * num, curr_min * num)
        curr_max, curr_min = max(candidates), min(candidates)
        result = max(result, curr_max)
    return result

title: Word Break
summary: Given a string and a dictionary of words, determine whether the string can be segmented into a sequence of one or more dictionary words.
approachSummary: A prefix of the string can be segmented into dictionary words exactly when some earlier breakpoint was itself segmentable and the substring from there to here is a dictionary word - a bottom-up boolean recurrence.
walkthrough: Build a boolean DP array where dp[i] means the first i characters can be segmented, with dp[0] = True (empty prefix). For each end position i, check every earlier breakpoint j where dp[j] is True and s[j:i] is in the word dictionary (as a set for O(1) lookup); if found, set dp[i] = True.

complexity.time: O(n^2) - n possible end positions, up to n possible breakpoints each, with O(1) substring hashing dominated by slicing cost
complexity.space: O(n) - the DP array, plus O(w) for the word set

pitfalls: Using a list instead of a set for the dictionary makes each 'in' check O(w) instead of O(1), degrading the whole algorithm
A pure recursive solution without memoization recomputes the same subproblems exponentially

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
summary: Given an integer array, find the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.
approachSummary: Maintaining a list of the smallest possible tail values for increasing subsequences of each length lets binary search find, for each new number, exactly where it extends or replaces a subsequence tail in O(log n).
walkthrough: Keep a list `tails` where tails[k] is the smallest tail value of any increasing subsequence of length k+1. For each number, binary search tails for the first position with a value >= the number; if found, replace that position (a better, smaller tail for that length); if not found, append it (extending the longest subsequence by one). The final list length is the answer.

complexity.time: O(n log n) - n numbers, each with a binary search over tails
complexity.space: O(n) - the tails list, bounded by the sequence length

pitfalls: A simpler O(n^2) DP (dp[i] = longest ending at i) is easier to reason about but slower - the tails-array trick with binary search is the O(n log n) optimization
The tails array does not represent an actual subsequence itself, only the best possible tail values - don't try to read the LIS directly from it

title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
summary: Given an integer array, determine whether it can be partitioned into two subsets with equal sums.
approachSummary: Splitting the array into two equal-sum halves is possible exactly when some subset sums to half the total, which is a classic 0/1 knapsack reachability question solvable with a boolean DP over achievable sums.
walkthrough: If the total sum is odd, it's immediately impossible. Otherwise target the total divided by two. Maintain a boolean set (or array) of achievable sums, starting with just 0. For each number, iterate achievable sums from high to low, adding number to each existing achievable sum to mark new sums reachable, avoiding reusing the same number twice in one pass.

complexity.time: O(n * target) - n numbers, each considering up to target possible sums
complexity.space: O(target) - the boolean DP array

pitfalls: Iterating achievable sums from low to high while updating in place lets a single number be used multiple times in the same subset - must iterate high to low
An odd total sum can never be split evenly - check this before running the DP

title: Unique Paths
summary: Given the dimensions of a grid, find how many unique paths there are from the top-left to the bottom-right, moving only right or down.
approachSummary: The number of ways to reach any cell moving only right or down is the sum of the ways to reach the cell above it and the cell to its left, since those are the only two cells that could lead into it.
walkthrough: Initialize a DP row representing the first row of the grid, all 1's (only one way to reach any cell in the top row - keep moving right). For each subsequent row, update each cell in place as the sum of the cell above (the current value before update) and the cell to the left (the just-updated value), keeping the row as a rolling 1D array.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every cell is computed once
complexity.space: O(n) - a single rolling row instead of the full 2D grid

pitfalls: A full 2D DP table works but a 1D rolling array is a simple space optimization since each row only depends on the row above
The first row and first column both only have exactly one path each - make sure they're initialized to 1, not 0

title: Longest Common Subsequence
summary: Given two strings, find the length of their longest common subsequence.
approachSummary: Two strings' longest common subsequence up to a given prefix pair either extends by one when the last characters match, or falls back to the better of dropping the last character of either string.
walkthrough: Build a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] is the LCS length of the first i characters of text1 and first j characters of text2. If the characters at positions i-1 and j-1 match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. Otherwise dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). The answer is in the bottom-right corner.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every cell of the DP table is computed once
complexity.space: O(m * n), reducible to O(min(m, n)) with a rolling array

pitfalls: Confusing this with the longest common SUBSTRING problem (which requires contiguous characters) leads to a different, simpler-looking but incorrect recurrence
Off-by-one indexing between the DP table (1-indexed conceptually) and the strings (0-indexed) is a common source of bugs

title: Best Time to Buy/Sell Stock With Cooldown
summary: Given daily stock prices, find the maximum profit from any number of trades, given a one-day cooldown required after selling before buying again.
approachSummary: Modeling each day as one of three states - holding a stock, in the cooldown period right after selling, or free to buy - and tracking the best profit achievable in each state lets transitions naturally encode the cooldown rule.
walkthrough: Track three running values: held (best profit while holding a stock), sold (best profit having just sold today), and rest (best profit being free to act, not just having sold). Each day, update: new held = max(held, rest - price), new sold = held + price, new rest = max(rest, sold). Iterate through all prices; the answer is the max of sold and rest at the end.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over the prices
complexity.space: O(1) - three running state variables

pitfalls: Forgetting that you can only buy from the 'rest' state, not directly after selling, is exactly what encodes the cooldown - conflating rest and sold breaks this
The final answer can't include 'held' since ending while still holding stock isn't optimal (you'd have unrealized profit)

title: Coin Change II
summary: Given coin denominations and a target amount, find how many distinct combinations of coins make up that amount.
approachSummary: Counting combinations (not permutations) that sum to the target requires processing one coin denomination fully before moving to the next, so each combination is only counted once regardless of the order coins are considered.
walkthrough: Build a DP array over amounts 0 to target, with dp[0] = 1. For each coin (outer loop), update dp[a] += dp[a - coin] for every amount a from the coin's value up to target (inner loop, ascending). Processing coins in the outer loop and amounts ascending in the inner loop ensures combinations aren't counted as different orderings.

complexity.time: O(amount * coins) - each coin considers every amount once
complexity.space: O(amount) - the DP array

pitfalls: Swapping the loop order (amounts outer, coins inner) counts permutations instead of combinations, overcounting the answer
This differs subtly from Coin Change I, which counts the minimum number of coins rather than the number of ways

title: Target SUm
summary: Given an array of integers and a target, find how many ways you can assign a '+' or '-' sign to each number so the expression evaluates to the target.
approachSummary: Assigning + or - to each number and hitting the target is equivalent to finding a subset P (the positives) whose sum satisfies sum(P) - (total - sum(P)) = target, which reduces to counting subsets summing to a fixed derived value.
walkthrough: Derive that sum(P) = (target + total) / 2 must be a non-negative integer; if not, return 0. Then count the number of subsets of nums that sum to that value using a DP over achievable sums, where dp[s] accumulates the number of ways to reach sum s, updated per number from high sums to low to avoid reuse.

complexity.time: O(n * total) - n numbers, each considering up to `total` possible sums
complexity.space: O(total) - the DP array of sum counts

pitfalls: A pure brute-force DFS over all +/- assignments is O(2^n) - the subset-sum reduction avoids that
(target + total) being odd, or its half exceeding the achievable sum range, both mean the answer is 0

title: Interleaving String
summary: Given three strings, determine whether the third can be formed by interleaving the first two while preserving each one's character order.
approachSummary: Whether a prefix of s3 can be formed by interleaving prefixes of s1 and s2 depends only on whether a shorter valid interleaving exists that this position extends, either by taking the next character from s1 or from s2.
walkthrough: If len(s1) + len(s2) != len(s3), it's immediately impossible. Build a 2D boolean DP table where dp[i][j] means the first i+j characters of s3 can be formed from the first i of s1 and first j of s2. dp[i][j] is true if (dp[i-1][j] and s1[i-1] matches s3[i+j-1]) or (dp[i][j-1] and s2[j-1] matches s3[i+j-1]).

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every cell of the DP table is computed once
complexity.space: O(m * n), reducible to O(min(m, n)) with a rolling array

pitfalls: Checking the length precondition first avoids wasted work and handles an immediate false case
Forgetting the base case dp[0][0] = True (empty prefixes trivially interleave) breaks the whole table

title: Longest Increasing Path in a Matrix
summary: Given an m x n matrix of integers, find the length of the longest path of strictly increasing values, moving between adjacent cells.
approachSummary: The longest increasing path starting at a cell is 1 plus the best among its strictly-greater neighbors' longest paths, and since that value only depends on the cell itself, memoizing it turns an exponential DFS into a linear one.
walkthrough: DFS from each cell, memoizing the longest increasing path length starting there. At each cell, recursively explore neighbors with strictly greater values, taking the best result plus one; memoize before returning so repeated calls to the same cell are O(1). The answer is the max memoized value across all cells.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - each cell's DFS result is computed exactly once thanks to memoization
complexity.space: O(m * n) - the memo table, plus recursion stack up to O(m * n) in a strictly increasing snake path

pitfalls: Without memoization, the DFS revisits the same cells repeatedly and becomes exponential
No visited set is needed since strictly increasing values naturally prevent revisiting a cell within one path (no cycles possible)

title: Distinct Subsequences
summary: Given two strings s and t, count how many distinct subsequences of s equal t.
approachSummary: The number of ways t's prefix appears as a subsequence of s's prefix is the ways achieved by skipping s's current character, plus (if the characters match) the ways achieved by matching them and advancing both prefixes.
walkthrough: Build a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] is the number of ways the first j characters of t appear as a subsequence of the first i characters of s, with dp[i][0] = 1 (empty t always matches) for all i. For each cell, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (skip s's character) plus dp[i-1][j-1] if s[i-1] == t[j-1] (also match it).

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every cell of the DP table is computed once
complexity.space: O(m * n), reducible to O(n) with a rolling row

pitfalls: dp[i][0] = 1 for every i is essential - it means an empty target string has exactly one way to appear (matching nothing)
Iterating the row updates in the wrong direction when using a 1D rolling array overwrites values still needed for the current row

title: Edit Distance
summary: Given two strings, find the minimum number of single-character insertions, deletions, and substitutions needed to turn the first into the second.
approachSummary: The edit distance between two prefixes is zero extra cost if their last characters match, or one plus the cheapest of insert, delete, or replace applied to the best solution for a slightly shorter prefix pair.
walkthrough: Build a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] is the edit distance between the first i characters of word1 and first j characters of word2, with the first row/column initialized to i and j (all inserts/deletes). If the current characters match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. Otherwise dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j] delete, dp[i][j-1] insert, dp[i-1][j-1] replace).

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every cell of the DP table is computed once
complexity.space: O(m * n), reducible to O(min(m, n)) with a rolling array

pitfalls: The base case row/column represent converting an empty string via all inserts or all deletes - they must be i and j respectively, not 0
Mixing up which operation corresponds to which neighboring cell (insert vs delete) is easy to get backwards

title: Burst Balloons
summary: Given balloons each with a number painted on it, find the maximum coins obtainable by bursting all of them, where bursting a balloon earns its value times its two current neighbors' values.
approachSummary: Thinking about which balloon is burst LAST within a range (rather than first) makes the two resulting sub-ranges independent, since that last balloon's neighbors at burst time are exactly the range's boundary balloons - a classic interval DP reframing.
walkthrough: Pad the balloon array with 1's at both ends. Build a DP table over open intervals (left, right), computing them in order of increasing interval width. For each interval, try every index k as the LAST balloon burst within it; the coins gained are nums[left] * nums[k] * nums[right] plus the best results of the two sub-intervals (left, k) and (k, right), already computed for smaller widths.

complexity.time: O(n^3) - O(n^2) intervals, each trying O(n) split points
complexity.space: O(n^2) - the interval DP table

pitfalls: Thinking about the FIRST balloon burst in a range makes the sub-problems dependent on each other (not independent), which is why the reframing to LAST burst is the key insight
Forgetting to pad both ends with virtual 1-valued balloons breaks the boundary multiplication for balloons at the true edges

title: Regular Expression Matching
summary: Given a string and a pattern with support for '.' and '*', implement regular expression matching that covers the entire input string.
approachSummary: Whether a prefix of s matches a prefix of the pattern depends on the pattern's next token: a plain character or '.' must match and advance both, while a '*' allows the preceding element to match zero times (skip it) or one-plus times (consume from s and stay on the same pattern position).
walkthrough: Build a 2D boolean DP table over positions in s and the pattern, filled from the end backward (or top-down with memoization). If the pattern character is followed by '*', the match is true if skipping the pair (star matches zero times) is true, or if the current characters match and the match with the same pattern position but next s position is true. Otherwise it's a direct character match (accounting for '.') combined with the next sub-problem.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every (s position, pattern position) pair is computed once
complexity.space: O(m * n) - the DP table, or recursion stack depth for a memoized recursive version

pitfalls: The '*' operator applies to the single element immediately preceding it, requiring lookahead/lookbehind handling that's easy to get backwards
Forgetting the zero-occurrence case for 'x*' (it can match an empty sequence) misses valid matches like pattern 'a*' against empty string

title: Maximum Subarray
summary: Given an integer array, find the contiguous subarray with the largest sum.
approachSummary: A running subarray sum should be discarded and restarted whenever it drops below zero, since a negative prefix can only ever hurt a future sum, which is the core insight of Kadane's algorithm.
walkthrough: Track a running sum starting at the first element. For each subsequent number, either extend the running sum by adding the number, or start fresh from the number alone if the running sum has gone negative (whichever is larger). Update a global best with the running sum at each step.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over the array
complexity.space: O(1) - two running variables

pitfalls: Resetting to 0 instead of restarting at the current number mishandles all-negative arrays, which must still return the least-negative single element
Divide-and-conquer is an alternative O(n log n) approach, but Kadane's linear scan is simpler and optimal

title: Jump Game
summary: Given an array where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position, determine whether you can reach the last index starting from the first.
approachSummary: Tracking the farthest index reachable so far while scanning left to right determines reachability of the last index without exploring every possible jump sequence.
walkthrough: Track the farthest reachable index, starting at 0. Walk through the array; if the current index exceeds the farthest reachable, the end is unreachable - return false. Otherwise update farthest reachable to the max of itself and index + nums[index]. If the loop completes, the last index was reachable.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over the array
complexity.space: O(1) - one running variable

pitfalls: A DP or BFS approach also works but is unnecessarily complex compared to this greedy scan
Checking reachability of the CURRENT index before updating the farthest bound catches unreachable gaps early

title: Jump Game II
summary: Given an array where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position, find the minimum number of jumps needed to reach the last index.
approachSummary: Treating each jump as expanding a 'level' of reachable indices, and greedily jumping only when the current level is exhausted, finds the minimum number of jumps like an implicit BFS over reachable ranges.
walkthrough: Track the current level's boundary (farthest reachable with jumps used so far) and the next level's farthest reachable. Walk through indices; update next-farthest with index + nums[index]. When the current index reaches the current level's boundary, increment the jump count and advance the boundary to next-farthest.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over the array
complexity.space: O(1) - a few running variables

pitfalls: Incrementing the jump count on every step instead of only at level boundaries overcounts jumps
Stopping the scan at the second-to-last index avoids an unnecessary final jump count increment when already at the end

title: Gas Station
summary: Given gas available and cost to travel between gas stations arranged in a circle, find the starting station from which you can complete the circuit, or determine it's impossible.
approachSummary: If the total gas is at least the total cost, a valid starting station is guaranteed to exist, and it must be right after the point where the running tank balance hits its lowest point, since any station before that would run out first.
walkthrough: Track a running total tank balance across the whole trip, and a separate running local balance since the last reset. Whenever the local balance goes negative, the candidate start can't be before the next station, so reset the local balance to 0 and move the candidate start to the next index. If the total tank balance across the whole trip is non-negative, the final candidate start is a valid answer.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over the stations
complexity.space: O(1) - a few running variables

pitfalls: A brute-force check of every starting station is O(n^2) - the single-pass greedy is the optimized approach
Forgetting to check the overall total is non-negative first means a candidate could be returned even when no valid start exists
solutionCode:
def can_complete_circuit(gas: list[int], cost: list[int]) -> int:
    total = 0
    local = 0
    start = 0
    for i in range(len(gas)):
        diff = gas[i] - cost[i]
        total += diff
        local += diff
        if local < 0:
            start = i + 1
            local = 0
    return start if total >= 0 else -1

title: Hand of Straights
summary: Given a hand of cards and a group size, determine whether the cards can be rearranged into groups of that size, each forming a run of consecutive values.
approachSummary: Always starting a new consecutive group from the smallest remaining card is safe because any larger card could equally start a group beginning at that smallest value, so greedily consuming groupSize consecutive cards from the current minimum never blocks a valid partition.
walkthrough: Count each card's frequency and sort the distinct values. For each value from smallest to largest, if it still has remaining count, that count must start that many new groups - consume one card of value, value+1, ..., value+groupSize-1 for each such group, decrementing their counts. If any required card is missing, it's impossible.

complexity.time: O(n log n) - dominated by sorting the distinct card values
complexity.space: O(n) - the frequency map

pitfalls: A min-heap can replace sorting for a similar effect, but sorting the distinct values is simpler here
Not checking that the total count is divisible by groupSize first can waste time before discovering it's impossible

title: Merge Triplets to Form Target Triplet
summary: Given an array of triplets and a target triplet, determine whether some subset of the triplets can be merged (taking the max of each coordinate) to exactly form the target.
approachSummary: A triplet is only useful for merging toward the target if none of its coordinates exceed the corresponding target coordinate, and among the usable triplets, the target is achievable exactly when each coordinate's target value is matched by at least one of them.
walkthrough: Filter to only triplets where every coordinate is <= the target's corresponding coordinate (any triplet with an over-large coordinate is useless since merging only takes maxes). Among the filtered triplets, track which of the three target coordinates have been exactly matched by some triplet. If all three are matched, the target is achievable.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over the triplets
complexity.space: O(1) - three boolean flags

pitfalls: Including a triplet with any coordinate exceeding the target's corresponding value would make merging overshoot that coordinate, since merge only takes the max
Checking exact equality (not >=) per coordinate is required since merging takes the max, so you need at least one triplet exactly matching each target value

title: Partition Labels
summary: Given a string, partition it into as many parts as possible so each letter appears in at most one part, and return the sizes of the parts.
approachSummary: A partition can only end at a position where no character within it reappears later, so extending the current partition's boundary to the last occurrence of every character seen so far, until reaching that boundary, produces the smallest valid partitions.
walkthrough: Precompute the last occurrence index of every character in the string. Walk through the string maintaining the current partition's end as the max of itself and the current character's last occurrence. When the current index reaches that end, the partition is complete - record its length and start a new partition.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass to build last-occurrence, another to build partitions
complexity.space: O(1) - the last-occurrence map is bounded by the alphabet size

pitfalls: Only extending the boundary based on the current character's last occurrence, forgetting to also account for all characters already included in the current partition, misses reappearances
The partition end check must use the current partition's start and end, not just check the whole string globally

title: Valid Parenthesis String
summary: Given a string containing '(', ')', and '*' (which can be '(', ')', or empty), determine whether it could be a valid parentheses string.
approachSummary: Tracking the range of possible counts of unmatched open parentheses - accounting for '*' being either '(', ')', or empty - lets a single pass determine validity without branching into every possible interpretation of each '*'.
walkthrough: Track a low and high bound on the number of currently unmatched open parentheses. On '(', increment both. On ')', decrement both. On '*', decrement low (treat as ')') and increment high (treat as '('), since it could be either. Clamp low at 0 (can't go negative - just treat some '*' as empty instead). If high ever drops below 0, too many ')' appeared - invalid. At the end, the string is valid if low can be 0.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over the string
complexity.space: O(1) - two running bounds

pitfalls: Trying all 3^k combinations of '*' interpretations is correct but exponential - the low/high range trick collapses this to linear
Clamping low at 0 (rather than letting it go negative) is essential since a negative low would incorrectly suggest more ')' are required than possible

title: Insert Interval
summary: Given a set of non-overlapping intervals sorted by start time and a new interval, insert it and merge any overlaps.
approachSummary: Since the existing intervals are already sorted and non-overlapping, the new interval only needs to be merged with the contiguous block of intervals it overlaps, leaving everything before and after untouched.
walkthrough: Walk through the intervals in three phases: first append every interval that ends entirely before the new interval starts (no overlap), then merge every interval that overlaps the new interval by expanding the new interval's bounds to cover them, append the merged result, and finally append every remaining interval that starts entirely after the new interval ends.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass through the intervals
complexity.space: O(n) - the result list

pitfalls: The overlap condition to keep merging is interval.start <= new_interval.end, not a stricter comparison
Forgetting to append the (possibly expanded) new interval itself before appending the remaining later intervals
solutionCode:
def insert(intervals: list[list[int]], new_interval: list[int]) -> list[list[int]]:
    result = []
    i = 0
    n = len(intervals)
    while i < n and intervals[i][1] < new_interval[0]:
        result.append(intervals[i])
        i += 1
    while i < n and intervals[i][0] <= new_interval[1]:
        new_interval = [min(new_interval[0], intervals[i][0]), max(new_interval[1], intervals[i][1])]
        i += 1
    result.append(new_interval)
    while i < n:
        result.append(intervals[i])
        i += 1
    return result

title: Merge Intervals
summary: Given an array of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals and return the resulting non-overlapping set.
approachSummary: Sorting intervals by start time ensures any two intervals that should merge become adjacent in the scan, so a single pass comparing each interval to the last merged one is enough to combine all overlaps.
walkthrough: Sort intervals by start value. Walk through them keeping a result list; for each interval, if it overlaps the last interval in the result (its start is <= the last one's end), merge them by extending the last interval's end. Otherwise append it as a new, separate interval.

complexity.time: O(n log n) - dominated by sorting
complexity.space: O(n) - the result list (or O(log n) to O(n) for the sort itself)

pitfalls: Comparing against the ORIGINAL previous interval instead of the last MERGED interval in the result misses chains of three or more overlapping intervals
Forgetting to sort first breaks the core assumption that overlapping intervals become adjacent

title: Non-Overlapping Intervals
summary: Given an array of intervals, find the minimum number of intervals to remove so the rest don't overlap.
approachSummary: Sorting intervals by their end time and greedily keeping the one that ends earliest whenever two overlap leaves the most room for future intervals, minimizing the total number removed.
walkthrough: Sort intervals by end value. Track the end of the last kept interval, starting at negative infinity. For each interval, if its start is before the last kept interval's end (overlap), it must be removed - increment a removal count. Otherwise keep it and update the last kept end to this interval's end.

complexity.time: O(n log n) - dominated by sorting
complexity.space: O(1) extra - excluding the sort's own space

pitfalls: Sorting by start time instead of end time doesn't guarantee the greedy choice is optimal - end time is the key to this problem
Comparing against the last KEPT interval's end, not the last interval seen overall, is what makes the greedy choice correct

title: Meeting Rooms
summary: Given an array of meeting time intervals, determine whether a single person could attend every meeting.
approachSummary: A person can attend all meetings exactly when no two meetings overlap, which sorting by start time and checking each meeting's start against the previous meeting's end reveals in a single pass.
walkthrough: Sort the intervals by start time. Walk through consecutive pairs; if any meeting's start time is before the previous meeting's end time, they overlap and the person can't attend both. If the whole pass completes without an overlap, return true.

complexity.time: O(n log n) - dominated by sorting
complexity.space: O(1) extra - excluding the sort's own space

pitfalls: Without sorting first, adjacent intervals in the input order don't necessarily reveal overlaps
The boundary case where one meeting ends exactly when another starts is not an overlap (back-to-back meetings are fine)

title: Meeting Rooms II
summary: Given an array of meeting time intervals, find the minimum number of meeting rooms required to hold them all.
approachSummary: The minimum number of rooms needed equals the maximum number of meetings simultaneously in progress at any moment, which can be tracked by scanning sorted start and end times separately as a running count of overlaps.
walkthrough: Sort start times and end times into two separate arrays. Walk through start times with two pointers, one for starts and one for ends. For each new meeting starting, if the earliest ending meeting so far has already ended (its end time <= this start time), reuse that room by advancing the end pointer; otherwise a new room is needed - increment a running room count and track the max seen.

complexity.time: O(n log n) - dominated by sorting both start and end arrays
complexity.space: O(n) - the separate sorted start/end arrays

pitfalls: A min-heap of end times is an equally valid alternative approach, tracking active meetings directly rather than counting via two pointers
Comparing with < instead of <= for room-reuse eligibility can incorrectly force a new room for back-to-back meetings

title: Minimum Interval to Include Each Query
summary: Given a list of intervals and a list of query points, find the size of the smallest interval containing each query point.
approachSummary: Processing queries in increasing order and, at each query, pushing all intervals that have started while lazily discarding intervals from a size-ordered min-heap that have already ended, always leaves the smallest still-covering interval at the heap's top.
walkthrough: Sort intervals by start and queries by value (keeping original indices to restore order later). Walk through queries in increasing order; push into a min-heap (keyed by interval size) every interval whose start is <= the current query. Then pop any heap-top intervals whose end is < the current query (expired). The heap's top interval size (or -1 if empty) is the answer for this query.

complexity.time: O((n + q) log n) - n intervals and q queries, each with a log n heap operation
complexity.space: O(n) - the heap can hold up to all intervals

pitfalls: Forgetting to lazily remove expired intervals from the heap top before reading the answer can return a stale, already-ended interval
Processing queries out of their sorted order breaks the assumption that intervals are only ever added, never re-checked, for earlier queries
solutionCode:
import heapq

def min_interval(intervals: list[list[int]], queries: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    intervals.sort()
    sorted_queries = sorted(range(len(queries)), key=lambda i: queries[i])
    result = [-1] * len(queries)
    heap: list[tuple[int, int]] = []
    i = 0
    for q_idx in sorted_queries:
        q = queries[q_idx]
        while i < len(intervals) and intervals[i][0] <= q:
            start, end = intervals[i]
            heapq.heappush(heap, (end - start + 1, end))
            i += 1
        while heap and heap[0][1] < q:
            heapq.heappop(heap)
        if heap:
            result[q_idx] = heap[0][0]
    return result

title: Rotate Image
summary: Given an n x n 2D matrix representing an image, rotate it 90 degrees clockwise in place.
approachSummary: Rotating a matrix 90 degrees clockwise is equivalent to first transposing it (swap rows and columns) and then reversing each row, both of which can be done in place.
walkthrough: Transpose the matrix in place by swapping matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i] for all i < j. Then reverse each row in place. The combination produces a 90-degree clockwise rotation without needing a second matrix.

complexity.time: O(n^2) - every cell is touched a constant number of times
complexity.space: O(1) - done entirely in place

pitfalls: Transposing without also reversing rows produces a mirror image rotation, not the correct clockwise rotation
Iterating the full matrix for the transpose swap (instead of just the upper triangle) swaps each pair twice, undoing the transpose

title: Spiral Matrix
summary: Given an m x n matrix, return all its elements visited in spiral order.
approachSummary: Tracking four shrinking boundaries - top, bottom, left, right - and walking each edge in turn (right along the top, down the right side, left along the bottom, up the left side) before shrinking the boundaries naturally traces the spiral.
walkthrough: Maintain top, bottom, left, right boundary indices. Repeat while top <= bottom and left <= right: walk right along the top row and then increment top; walk down the right column and then decrement right; if top <= bottom, walk left along the bottom row and decrement bottom; if left <= right, walk up the left column and increment left.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every cell is visited exactly once
complexity.space: O(1) extra, excluding the output list

pitfalls: Forgetting the guard checks (top <= bottom, left <= right) before the third and fourth edges causes cells to be revisited or indices to go out of bounds on non-square matrices
Not shrinking a boundary immediately after finishing an edge causes infinite loops or duplicate visits

title: Set Matrix Zeroes
summary: Given an m x n matrix, if an element is 0, set its entire row and column to 0, in place.
approachSummary: Using the matrix's own first row and first column as marker space to record which rows and columns contain a zero avoids needing separate O(m + n) tracking arrays, at the cost of handling the first row/column's own zero status separately.
walkthrough: First check whether the first row and first column originally contain any zero (save as flags). Then scan the rest of the matrix; whenever a zero is found at (i, j), mark matrix[i][0] and matrix[0][j] as zero (as markers). Scan again, zeroing out any cell whose row-marker or column-marker is zero (skipping row/col 0). Finally, zero out the first row/column themselves based on the saved flags.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - a constant number of passes over the matrix
complexity.space: O(1) - uses the matrix itself as marker storage instead of extra arrays

pitfalls: Zeroing cells during the same pass that's still reading original values corrupts the markers before they're fully collected
Forgetting to handle the first row/column's own original zero status separately, since they double as marker storage, causes them to be mishandled

title: Happy Number
summary: Given a number, determine whether it is 'happy' - repeatedly replacing it with the sum of the squares of its digits eventually reaches 1.
approachSummary: Repeatedly replacing a number with the sum of the squares of its digits either reaches 1 or falls into a cycle, and Floyd's cycle detection (fast/slow pointers) can determine which happens without a hash set.
walkthrough: Define a step function that computes the sum of squares of a number's digits. Run slow and fast pointers starting at the input, where slow takes one step per iteration and fast takes two. If fast reaches 1, the number is happy. If slow and fast ever meet at a value other than 1, a cycle exists and the number isn't happy.

complexity.time: O(log n) per digit-square-sum step, bounded overall by the cycle length which is small and constant for this problem
complexity.space: O(1) with the fast/slow pointer approach, versus O(cycle length) for a hash-set approach

pitfalls: A hash-set-of-seen-values approach is simpler to write and equally valid, just uses more memory than the pointer trick
Forgetting the digit-sum-of-squares can itself require multiple digit extractions per step, which is easy to implement incorrectly with modulo/division order

title: Plus One
summary: Given a non-negative integer represented as an array of digits, add one to the number and return the resulting digit array.
approachSummary: Adding one to a number represented as a digit array only needs to ripple a carry leftward through trailing 9's, since any other last digit simply increments without affecting earlier digits.
walkthrough: Walk the digits from the last to the first. If a digit is less than 9, increment it and return immediately - no further carry is needed. If it's 9, set it to 0 and continue the carry leftward. If the loop finishes (all digits were 9), prepend a 1 to the front of the array.

complexity.time: O(n) - at most one pass through the digits
complexity.space: O(1) extra, or O(n) if a new array must be allocated for the all-9's case

pitfalls: Converting to an integer, adding one, and converting back works for small inputs but can overflow or be inefficient for arbitrarily long digit arrays
Forgetting the all-9's edge case (e.g. [9,9,9] -> [1,0,0,0]) which requires growing the array

title: Pow(x, n)
summary: Implement pow(x, n), which calculates x raised to the power n.
approachSummary: Repeatedly squaring the base and halving the exponent computes x^n in logarithmic time instead of multiplying x by itself n times, using the binary representation of the exponent implicitly.
walkthrough: Handle a negative exponent by inverting x and negating n. Recursively (or iteratively) compute: if n is 0, return 1; if n is even, return (x squared) raised to n/2; if n is odd, return x times (x squared) raised to (n-1)/2. Each step halves the exponent.

complexity.time: O(log n) - the exponent is halved at each step
complexity.space: O(log n) recursion stack, or O(1) if implemented iteratively

pitfalls: A naive loop multiplying x by itself n times is O(n), far slower than the O(log n) fast exponentiation
Integer overflow of n when negating a very negative n (like INT_MIN) needs care in languages with fixed-width integers, though not in Python

title: Multiply Strings
summary: Given two non-negative integers represented as strings, return the product of the two numbers, also as a string.
approachSummary: Multiplying two numbers digit by digit, like elementary school long multiplication, and accumulating partial products into a result array indexed by digit position avoids ever converting to a native integer type.
walkthrough: Create a result array of size len(num1) + len(num2), initialized to zero. For every pair of digits (i from num1, j from num2), multiply them and add the product into result[i+j+1], then propagate any carry into result[i+j]. After processing all pairs, convert the result array to a string, stripping any leading zeros.

complexity.time: O(m * n) - every pair of digits is multiplied once
complexity.space: O(m + n) - the result digit array

pitfalls: Converting both strings to integers directly defeats the purpose of the problem, which is typically meant to avoid relying on big-integer support
Forgetting to strip leading zeros from the final result (e.g. multiplying by '0') can leave an incorrect leading-zero string
solutionCode:
def multiply(num1: str, num2: str) -> str:
    if num1 == '0' or num2 == '0':
        return '0'
    m, n = len(num1), len(num2)
    result = [0] * (m + n)
    for i in range(m - 1, -1, -1):
        for j in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
            mul = int(num1[i]) * int(num2[j])
            p1, p2 = i + j, i + j + 1
            total = mul + result[p2]
            result[p2] = total % 10
            result[p1] += total // 10
    result_str = ''.join(map(str, result)).lstrip('0')
    return result_str

title: Detect Squares
summary: Design a data structure that adds points and counts the number of axis-aligned squares that can be formed using an added point and three previously added points.
approachSummary: Given one corner of a candidate axis-aligned square, the other three corners are fully determined by the side length, so counting points sharing an x-coordinate or y-coordinate with the query point and checking the corresponding diagonal points in a frequency map finds all valid squares.
walkthrough: Maintain a frequency count of all added points. For a query point, iterate over every stored point sharing the same y-coordinate to determine a candidate side length. For each such point, compute the two other corners needed to complete an axis-aligned square, and multiply the frequencies of the same-row point, and the two computed corner points, adding the product to a running total.

complexity.time: O(n) per count query - iterating points sharing the query's row; O(1) for add
complexity.space: O(n) - the frequency map of all added points

pitfalls: Squares must be axis-aligned per the problem, which simplifies corner computation to simple x/y offsets rather than general rotated squares
Forgetting to exclude the degenerate case where the candidate same-row point has the same x as the query point (zero side length)

title: Single Number
