summary: Given a non-empty array of integers where every element appears twice except for one, find that single element.
approachSummary: XOR-ing every number together cancels out every value that appears twice (since x XOR x = 0), leaving only the value that appears once.
walkthrough: Initialize a result to 0. XOR every number in the array into it. Since XOR is commutative and associative, all paired values cancel to 0, and 0 XOR the unpaired value leaves just that value.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over the array
complexity.space: O(1) - a single accumulator

pitfalls: A hash-set-based frequency count also works but uses O(n) space versus XOR's O(1)
This trick specifically relies on every OTHER number appearing exactly twice - it doesn't generalize directly to three-or-more duplicates without modification

title: Number of 1 Bits
summary: Given an unsigned integer, return the number of '1' bits it has (its Hamming weight).
approachSummary: The expression n & (n - 1) always clears the lowest set bit of n, so counting how many times this operation can be applied before n reaches zero directly counts the number of set bits.
walkthrough: Initialize a count to 0. While n is nonzero, apply n = n & (n - 1) to clear its lowest set bit, and increment the count. Once n reaches 0, the count equals the number of 1 bits originally in n.

complexity.time: O(k) - k is the number of set bits, which is at most the bit width
complexity.space: O(1) - a single counter

pitfalls: Shifting and checking every one of the 32 bits individually also works but is slightly slower than only iterating once per set bit
In Python, negative numbers have an infinite two's-complement representation conceptually, so this trick assumes a non-negative or fixed-width unsigned input as the problem specifies

title: Counting Bits
summary: Given an integer n, return an array where each index i holds the number of 1 bits in the binary representation of i, for all i from 0 to n.
approachSummary: The number of set bits in i equals the set bits in i with its lowest set bit removed, plus one, so each answer can be built from a previously computed smaller answer instead of recounting from scratch.
walkthrough: Build a DP array of size n+1, with dp[0] = 0. For each i from 1 to n, compute dp[i] = dp[i & (i - 1)] + 1, reusing the already-computed bit count for i with its lowest set bit cleared.

complexity.time: O(n) - each value is computed in O(1) using a previous result
complexity.space: O(n) - the output array

pitfalls: Recomputing each value's bit count independently (e.g. with the Number of 1 Bits trick per value) is correct but redundant - the DP relation reuses prior work
i & (i - 1) is always a smaller number than i, guaranteeing the DP lookup is always into an already-computed cell

title: Reverse Bits
summary: Given a 32-bit unsigned integer, reverse its bits.
approachSummary: Building the result one bit at a time by repeatedly shifting the result left and appending the input's lowest bit reverses a fixed-width integer's bit order.
walkthrough: Initialize result to 0. For each of the 32 bit positions, shift result left by one to make room, then OR in the current lowest bit of the input (n & 1), and shift the input right by one to expose the next bit. After 32 iterations, result holds the bit-reversed value.

complexity.time: O(1) - always exactly 32 iterations for a fixed-width integer
complexity.space: O(1) - a single accumulator

pitfalls: Forgetting that this problem is specifically for a fixed 32-bit width - the loop must run exactly 32 times regardless of how many bits are actually set
Using Python's arbitrary-precision integers without masking can cause unexpected sign or width issues if not careful with the shift operations

title: Missing Number
summary: Given an array containing n distinct numbers from the range 0 to n, find the one number missing from the range.
approachSummary: XOR-ing every index from 0 to n together with every value in the array cancels out every value that's actually present, leaving only the one missing number.
walkthrough: Initialize a result to n (the highest possible index). XOR in every index i from 0 to n-1 and every value nums[i] from the array. Every present number cancels with its matching index XOR, leaving the missing number as the final result.

complexity.time: O(n) - one pass over the array
complexity.space: O(1) - a single accumulator

pitfalls: Summing 0 to n and subtracting the actual sum of the array is an equally valid and arguably simpler alternative to XOR here
Forgetting to include n itself (the extra index beyond the array's length) in the XOR misses the case where n itself is the missing number

title: Sum of Two Integers
summary: Given two integers, calculate their sum without using the '+' or '-' operators.
approachSummary: Addition without the + operator can be simulated with XOR for the sum-without-carry and AND-then-shift for the carry, repeating until there's no carry left to propagate.
walkthrough: Repeatedly compute a tentative sum as a XOR b (adding without carrying) and a carry as (a AND b) shifted left by one (the bits that need to carry over). Set a to the tentative sum and b to the carry, and repeat until the carry (b) is zero, at which point a holds the final sum.

complexity.time: O(1) - bounded by the fixed bit width of the integers (32 iterations at most)
complexity.space: O(1) - a couple of accumulator variables

pitfalls: Python integers are arbitrary precision, so negative numbers and overflow need explicit 32-bit masking to match the problem's expected fixed-width integer behavior
Forgetting to mask to 32 bits can cause the loop to run indefinitely or produce an incorrectly signed result for negative inputs

title: Reverse Integer
summary: Given a signed 32-bit integer, reverse its digits, returning 0 if the reversed value overflows the 32-bit signed range.
approachSummary: Repeatedly peeling off the last digit with modulo and appending it to a running reversed result, while checking bounds before each multiply, reverses the digits of an integer while respecting a fixed 32-bit signed range.
walkthrough: Track the sign separately and work with the absolute value. Repeatedly extract the last digit via x % 10, append it to a running result via result = result * 10 + digit, and remove that digit from x via integer division by 10. Before each multiply, check whether it would overflow the 32-bit signed range, and return 0 immediately if so. Reapply the sign at the end.

complexity.time: O(log n) - one iteration per digit of the input
complexity.space: O(1) - a few accumulator variables

pitfalls: Checking for overflow only after the multiplication happens can already have caused undefined or wrapped behavior in fixed-width languages - check before multiplying
Python's integers don't naturally overflow, so the 32-bit boundary check (-2^31 to 2^31 - 1) must be done explicitly to match the problem's constraints
solutionCode:
def reverse(x: int) -> int:
    sign = -1 if x < 0 else 1
    x = abs(x)
    result = 0
    int_max = 2**31 - 1
    while x != 0:
        digit = x % 10
        x //= 10
        if result > (int_max - digit) // 10:
            return 0
        result = result * 10 + digit
    result *= sign
    return result if -2**31 <= result <= int_max else 0
